codewordLabels.COVEN-WARD: a ward kept in your fist
codewordLabels.COVEN-INGENUE-FREED: you helped her toward the death she wanted

blocks.s: You come up the dark drive on foot, gravel gone soft with rain, and the house is lit from cellar to attic, every window a square of gold, and the cold of the night ends the moment you step under the portico. Warmth comes off the door before you touch it. There is music from somewhere deep inside, a string quartet that never quite reaches the end of its phrase. There is candle smoke and hothouse lilies and, under the lilies, something sweeter that you do not let yourself name yet.
You have been here before. You did not wander up out of the weather. You came back, on purpose, for someone who is kept in this house, far down under the music, and you mean to reach them tonight.

In the warm dark of the porch your right hand finds the curved back of a chair set out by the door and rests there, holding the shape of a seat that no one is sitting in. You did not decide to do that. You take the hand back.

lines: THE CURATOR, low, from the brass of the bell-pull, before you ring it:
Welcome back. We kept the lamps on. Some of us never could stay away from a house like this, either.

choices.label: Cross to the door, where a thin man waits
Hang back on the porch and read the house first

gated.aside: You did the hard thing once, the unthinkable thing, and you have carried the cost of it ever since. The coven knows. This is the house where you never have to do it again. Come in. Nothing is ever let go here.
Ah. You kept yours. You held on past the point where holding on was kind, and you would do it again. The coven has been waiting for one of its own. You are already halfway to the table.
You stop on the warm threshold and you count, one, two, the way you always count before a door, and the house counts with you, patient, in no hurry at all. It has all the time you do not.
The warmth reaches for you before the Usher does. You have learned, somewhere, to distrust a welcome that arrives that fast.

blocks.s: You set the cup back on the tray, full. It is harder than it should be. Your hand wants to keep it; the warm room wills it back into your fingers; the servant's patient unspeaking face makes you feel, for a moment, like a guest refusing bread in someone's home.

You refuse it anyway. The tightness you came in carrying stays where it is, in your shoulders and your jaw, and you are glad of it, because it is yours, and it is the shape of the thing you came to do, and you do not want it loosened out of you by good wine and an iron undertaste you would rather not think about.
The servant withdraws. The room cools toward you by a degree it would deny. You keep your edge.

choices.label: Go back into the salon, clear-headed
Go toward the stair down

blocks.s: "That," he says, nodding at the rosary, "belonged to a woman who came up the drive for her husband and would not drink and would not be soothed, and walked out the other side of this house with him in a box and grief in her hands and her whole mortal life still ahead of her to spend on missing him." The latch clicks, soft. "She is the only one I ever opened the far door for. I keep this passage cold so I do not forget there is a far door."

He does not show it to you. "Not tonight. You have not seen what you came to see; the far door is no use to you until you have. But you carry her beads and you would not be soothed, and that is the start of it. Go down. When the time comes, find me at a cold door, not a warm one." The latch clicks. "Mind the step."

choices.label: Go back to the salon and toward the stair
Go straight to the stair down

blocks.s: "Have we met," he repeats, not quite a question back, and the latch clicks again under his thumb. "I have worked this door a long time. I let people in. That is the whole of the job and it is more of a job than it sounds."
He looks at you properly then, and the practised courtesy thins for a second over something tired underneath.

"I was at this door the night you said yes. I held it for you both going in. I have held it for you coming back more than once since." The latch clicks. "I am not supposed to remember that. I remember it anyway. Mind the step."

choices.label: Ask him what he means by the night you said yes
Step over the threshold

gated.aside: He meets your eyes a beat too long, one doorman recognising another, the small click of his latch arriving just before his courtesy, and for a moment he forgets which of you is meant to be admitting whom.

blocks.s: A servant you did not see approach is at your elbow with a tray, and on the tray is one cup, only one, as if it had been poured for you in particular and carried through the whole crowded room to find you. It is good wine, dark and warm, and there is a smell coming off it of cherries and oak and, under that, low and unmistakable, a thread of iron, the smell of a cut thumb, of a coin held in a hot hand.

The servant says nothing. Waits. Around you the party watches without watching, glasses lifted to mouths and never tipped, and you understand that this is the moment the room has been arranging itself toward since you came in out of the rain. Drink, and be one of them, and be warm. Or do not.

choices.label: Drink
Set the cup back down on the tray
Refuse it and say aloud why you are here

gated.aside: Do not drink. The welcome is the binding. Comfort is just the slow way this house says stay.
Your hand is already warm around it. You are a person who holds on, and a full cup is only one more thing to hold.

blocks.s: You let it hold you. You have, in fact, done enough. You have done years of enough, alone, and no one has ever once told you to stop, and now someone warm and kind is telling you to stop, and you find you have been waiting your whole life to be given permission.

The furs settle. The fires hold their heat at exactly blood temperature on either side of you. Guests drift past and touch your shoulder, welcome home, and one by one the lamps are turned down, not out, never out, only down, to the soft amber of a room where someone is sleeping and must not be disturbed. The stair stays at the far end of the salon, patient, unused. The one you came for stays down it, kept, uncome-for, while up here, on the long warm couch, you become at last what the house most wanted you to be, which is comfortable, and which is staying.

Just before the stair there is a small warm sitting room, lamplit, empty, a fire laid and lit for no one, a single deep chair turned to face it. You will only sit a moment. You have been on your feet since the rain. You will rest, and gather yourself, and then you will go down with your wits about you instead of staggering down tired.

The chair takes you in like the door did. The fire is the perfect heat. On a small table at your elbow a glass has been poured, ruby and warm, just in case, and a folded blanket waits over the arm. The room was made for exactly this, for a person who has decided to go down and would like, very reasonably, to be comfortable for one moment first.
The stair is just outside. You will go in a moment. The chair is so very kind.

choices.label: Make yourself get up at once and take the stair
Rest just a little; close your eyes for a moment

blocks.s: Off the hall is a cloakroom, dim and unlit, crowded with the coats of two hundred years of guests, fur and wool and oilskin and good dark cashmere, all of it kept, none of it thrown out. It is the one cool room you have found. The party's warmth does not reach in here, and you can hear yourself think.

You go along the rail by feel. One coat, old tweed, holds the smell of cigarettes so strongly that for a moment a whole person stands up in your mind, a real one, somebody who smoked too much and laughed wet and is presumably long dead in the ordinary way the people in this house are not. In the pocket of that coat your fingers find a string of beads with a small cross on the end, worn smooth, a rosary somebody kept and somebody else forgot to take when they were kept.

choices.label: Take the rosary and keep it on you
Leave it; you do not want what comes with refusing comfort

blocks.s: You crouch. It is them. The painter caught the thing the camera in your phone never could, the particular way they used to tilt their head when they thought you were being ridiculous and loved you for it. The background is a summer you remember the smell of. There is grass. There is glare. There is a thumb half over the corner of the painted scene, as if it had been worked from a photograph and the painter had faithfully copied even the thumb of whoever took it.

The thumb is yours. You took this picture. It was a Tuesday, you think, an ordinary unbearable Tuesday before any of this, and you have it still, the real one, folded in a pocket you have been pretending you did not bring.

choices.label: Take out the real photograph and keep it on you
Leave the photo where it is; you cannot look at it now

blocks.s: Beside the small portrait there is a gap on the wall exactly the size of a second small frame, a clean rectangle where the wallpaper is unfaded, where something has hung long enough to keep the wall behind it new. A hook is still there. Nothing hangs from it.

A guest drifts up beside you, glass in hand, warm and confiding. "That space," she says, fond, "that is for the one who keeps. We hang the kept, and beside each one we leave the keeper's place, against the day they come and stay and we can fill it. Yours has been waiting a long while." She smiles at you, glad. "It is the loveliest custom. No one is ever quite finished here. No one is ever quite gone."
Your reflection in the glass over the gap is wearing your coat with the collar turned wrong on one side. You look round. There is no mirror there. It is just the dark glaze of the empty wall.

choices.label: Look at the small portrait instead
Step back from the wall

blocks.s: You ask the way down, to the one you came for, and the voice answers from a different corner now, closer.

"...the one you came for," it says. "Down. It is always down. Past the table, past the long feast, past where the eldest sleep. The house will let you go down; it does not mind you going down. What it minds is your going down with all of yourself still in your pockets." A breath that is not a breath. "Drink and you go lighter. Refuse the cup and you keep your edges and the stairs are harder and longer. Either way the stair is at the far end. You knew that. I only said it first."

choices.label: Go to the tray and the cup
Go to the stair
Go back into the salon

blocks.s: You rest, just a little, just your eyes, just until the fire stops being so bright behind your lids. The chair holds you. The blanket is the right weight. The glass at your elbow is there if you want it and you find, drifting, that you do, and you drink without quite waking, and the warmth pours down into you and pools.

When you open your eyes again, or think you do, the stair is gone from the doorway. There is only more warm room beyond, and more after that, an endless gentle indoors, and a servant is drawing the curtains against a dawn you will not now see. You meant to go down. You will, you tell yourself, when you have rested. The chair has already learned your shape. It will keep it for you, faithfully, forever, in case you ever do get up. You will not get up. That is what the chair was for.

You step over, and the house takes you in. There is no other way to say it. The warmth closes around you like water at body heat, neither hot nor cool, exactly the temperature of nothing, and the door does not so much shut behind you as simply stop being a way out you were thinking about.

The thin man inclines his head. "You are welcome here," he says, and it is not small talk; it is a thing being done to you, a latch turning over somewhere you cannot see. "You were always welcome here. That has never been the difficulty."

The music is louder now, and the lily-smell, and under the lily-smell the sweet heavy note you would not name on the porch, which is the smell of flowers a few days past their best in a room that is never aired.

choices.label: Go through into the lit salon
Ask, before you go in, whether you can refuse all this

blocks.s: There is, of course, a second cup. There is always a second cup. The servant is there with it before you have finished wanting it, and you take it, because the first was so good and the warmth so complete and the night so long, and you drink.

Somewhere between the bottom of the cup and setting it down you lose the thread. There was a reason you came. There was a stair, and someone at the bottom of it, and a creased photograph against your chest, and a hard thing you came to do. The reason is here somewhere. You will find it again in a minute. There is no hurry. There is a chair, just there, warm from the fire, and someone is already pulling it out for you, and it would be so rude, now, after such a welcome, not to sit.

choices.label: Sit down, just for a minute
Put the cup down and walk, before the chair

blocks.s: You sit, just for a minute, because after such a welcome it would be rude not to. The second cup did its quiet work; the chair finishes it. The warmth comes up through the seat and the fur and the fire and meets the warmth already in you and they are the same warmth now and you cannot find the seam.

There was a reason you came. You are almost sure of it. A stair, perhaps. A face in a small low frame. Something folded in your inside pocket that you keep meaning to look at. It will come to you. There is no hurry; everyone says so, and everyone is so kind, and they are all so very glad you are finally home and finally still and finally not going anywhere.

A servant comes to take your empty cup and pauses, and turns over the little card at your place on the side table so the name faces down, a card turned for someone who has sat and will not be getting up. You do not see them do it. You have stopped seeing most things. You are warm. You are home. You stay.
You ask about the door she is leaning on, and she goes still in a way she has not yet, all the dry gone out of her.

"It's a child's room," she says. "There's a child in it who's been a child a very long time. I sit here because somebody should sit outside a child's door, and nobody else in this house will, because to sit here you have to admit there's something wrong with a child who doesn't grow." She tips her head back against the wood. "Don't go in. Not tonight. You're not ready, and neither is the kid, and there's a thing in there with a balloon that'll ask you a question you can't answer yet."
From the far side of the door, faint, comes the small dull tap of a balloon nosing the wood.

choices.label: Respect that, and go back down
Try the handle anyway

blocks.s: "What I mean," he says, and for once he does not have a ready courtesy to set in front of it. "You came up this drive once with someone who was dying. The ordinary way. The unstoppable way. You could not bear it. They offered you a way that they would not have to stop, and you said yes, and I opened the door, and you both went in, and one of you came back out and one of you did not."
The latch clicks, small.

"I do not blame you. I have stood at this door a long time and I have never once seen anyone say no to that offer with the right person dying in their arms. I only tell you so that when the house tells you, later, all warm and kind, that you did a loving thing, you will already know it told you the truth. The truth is the bait here. Mind the step."

choices.label: Step over the threshold

blocks.s: One whole wall of the salon is hung with portraits, frame to frame, floor to ceiling, and every face in them is here in the room tonight, older in the paint and unchanged in the flesh, the guests and their grandparents and their grandparents wearing the same too-still smile across two hundred years of fashion. A family that does not thin out the way families do.

Low on the wall, where you would have to crouch to see it well, is a smaller frame, and the face in it stops you. You know it. You knew it before this house. Painted younger and warmer than you ever managed to keep them in your own head, in daylight, outdoors, squinting a little into a real sun.

choices.label: Crouch and look closely at the small portrait
Look at the empty space beside it, the size of a second frame
Step back from the wall before it has you

blocks.s: You close your hand around the beads and the little cross presses into your palm and the smell of someone's cigarettes follows you out of the cool room into the warm one, and the warm room notices.

It is nothing you could point to. A guest's smile holds a half-second too long before it finds you. The over-warm air seems to keep a small distance now, the way a room keeps a small distance from a person who has been crying. The fires burn no lower but you feel a degree less wanted, and the relief of being less wanted, here, is enormous.
You have made yourself a little harder to soothe. That was the price and you have paid it gladly.

choices.label: Go back into the salon, warded
Go toward the stair down

blocks.s: "What do I want." She considers you, deciding whether you can be told. "I want to be tired and have it count for something. I want to be able to use up. Everyone I ever loved is going to get old and stop and I am going to sit here being twenty-two and lovely forever, flinching, and they call that a gift."
She stops. She has said more than she meant to and she does not take it back.

"You came for somebody. I can tell. Whatever you do down there, don't let them tell you keeping a person and saving a person are the same word. They worked very hard on me to make me think they were." Then, dry again, armour back up: "Anyway. Lovely chat. You should go before someone warm and friendly comes to fetch you."

choices.label: Promise her nothing, but mean it; go down

blocks.s: A staircase curves up out of the salon toward the bedroom floors, and you climb a few steps into cooler, dimmer air, away from the party, because you can hear someone up there who is not at the party. A young woman, somewhere along the unlit landing, humming. Three notes, rising, the same three, over and over, the way a person hums to keep from listening to something else.

You go up to the turn of the stair and there she is, sat on the floor with her back to a closed door, knees up, in a beautiful dress she clearly did not choose. She stops humming when she sees you and looks you over with the frank unimpressed eye of someone too tired to be polite.
"Oh good," she says. "A new one. They do love a new one. Don't get comfortable, it's catching."

choices.label: Ask her why she will not go down to the party
Ask her about the closed door at her back
Leave her to it and go back down

blocks.s: Behind a fall of red velvet there is a small door you would not have found if the house had not, in welcoming you, given you the run of itself. It opens to your hand because you are invited; an uninvited hand, you understand without being told, would have found only wall.

Inside is a small warm parlour set for two, a low fire, a decanter, two chairs drawn close, and one of the chairs is occupied by something wearing your coat. It has your build and your way of sitting and the collar is turned under wrong on the side you would never turn it. It does not look up. It is practising the way you hold a cup. It is getting better at it. There is a second chair, pulled out, warm, waiting, and it is the most comfortable-looking chair you have ever seen.

choices.label: Sit down across from the thing in your coat
Back out and pull the curtain shut

23.title: The Chair by the Fire (an ending)

blocks.s: You let them lead you to the fire, just to sit, just for a minute, just until your legs are under you and the room stops being so far off. The chair is warm already, warmed for you, and someone tucks a fur across your knees, and someone else refills the glass you do not remember being given, and the music goes on not finishing in a way that is, you find, deeply restful.

You will go down in a minute. You will. There is someone down there. You hold the thought of them carefully, the way you hold a coal, and the fire is so warm that the coal in your hands is hard to feel as separate from it, and the photograph against your chest is just a folded bit of stiffness you keep meaning to take out and look at, later, when you are less tired.

Later is a word this house can do a great deal with. You stay the night. You will go down tomorrow. Above you, behind a closed pale door, a child taps a balloon against the wood, and on the landing a girl hums three rising notes and does not come down to tell you to run, because she has learned that by the time they are in the chair they cannot hear her.

You step down off the lip of the light, and then again, and the warmth lets go of you slowly, reluctantly, the way warm water lets go of a hand drawn out of it. By the fourth step the music is behind you and above you. By the tenth it is only a suggestion in the stone. The cold here is not the dead frost of some other house you half-remember; it is the cool of a cellar, of stored things, of a place kept at a steady temperature for keeping's sake.
A latch clicks behind you, on the stair. The thin man has come down a few steps after you, not to stop you, only to see.

"Down, then," he says, with something in it that might be respect and might be grief. "They mostly do not. They mostly cannot leave the warm. Go on. Mind the step. It is a long way to the table, and the table is only the start of the way down."

choices.label: Go down toward the long table

blocks.s: You turn around on the warm side of the door and you walk back out, off the step, down the soft wet gravel, into the November dark, because you will not be welcomed into a thing like this, not even for them.

The cold takes you at once, and it is only ordinary cold, rain and wind and a long dark drive, and you are glad of it for about a hundred yards. Then the drive does not end. The gate you came in by is not where you left it. The lit house stays exactly the same distance behind you however long you walk, golden, warm, patient, every window full of people who are not cold and never will be, watching the one cold thing on the grounds get colder.

You could go back. The door is open; it is always open; the warmth is right there. You only have to stop being the one cold thing. You walk until you cannot, and then you stand, and the house waits, because the house has all the time in the world, and you, out here, find you have rather less of it than you thought.

You take the photograph out and hold it in the candlelight. It is creased white where you have folded and unfolded it. The sun in it is the real sun, the one outside this house, the one that ages things and ends them, and the people in the warm room nearby will not look at it; one of them turns her too-still face away as if the small bright square gave off a draught.

You put it back in your inside pocket, against your chest, and button the pocket. It is not much. It is a piece of the daylight world, where people die and that is allowed, and you have decided to carry it down into a house where they do not.

choices.label: Go back into the salon
Go toward the stair down

27.title: Let Me Be the Keeper (an ending)

blocks.s: You stay in the chair. You let the thing across the fire have your face and your place and the hard thing you came to do, because it is so reasonable, because you are so tired, because someone has finally offered to carry it and you have been carrying it alone for longer than this house has had a word for time.

It rises, wearing you, the collar of your coat righting itself on your shoulders as it learns the last of you. It pats your hand, kindly, with your own hand. "Rest," it says, in your voice, now exactly your voice. "I will keep them. I will keep them so well. You never have to choose again." And it goes down the stair you could not face, to be the keeper, to sit the vigil, to never once let the one you love finally rest, because that is what keeping is, and you taught it how.

You stay in the chair by the fire, faceless now, comfortable, kept. Some nights, far down, you hear it humming three rising notes in your voice, and you cannot remember why the sound should frighten you.
You ask the voice its name, and there is a pause, the only pause it has given you, because for once you have asked it something it cannot reach ahead and find.

"...my name," it says, finishing the question, not answering it. "I had one. I have looked for it the way you look for a word on your tongue, for what would be years if there were years here. It is gone. They keep you so long and so comfortably that the edges of you go first, the name, then the face, then the difference between your own thought and the next person's." A flat, awful evenness. "That is the kindness of the place. It takes nothing from you all at once. It only keeps you, and keeping is enough."

choices.label: Ask it the way down
Ask it about the one who runs the house
Go back to the salon, unsettled

gated.aside: It says your next word a half-beat before you do, flat, tired, and you know that flatness from the inside. You have spent yourself on other people's cues until you forgot the sound of your own name too.

blocks.s: The quartet is in here, four players in good black, and they are not playing. They sit with their instruments raised and their bows on the strings and they hold it, the breath before the note, and the music you have been hearing comes from somewhere that is not them, from the corners, from the dark gloss of the piano lid, from behind the curtains.
A voice comes out of one of the corners, low and flat, with no face attached to it. It is saying the end of your own thought before you finish having it.

"...behind the curtains, yes," it says, as you think the words behind the curtains. "You always look behind the curtains. There is nothing there but the wall and me, and I am not much to look at any more. I have been here so long I have mislaid the part of me that had a face."

choices.label: Ask the voice who it used to be
Ask the voice the way down to the one you came for
Ask the voice about the one who runs the house
Leave it to its corner and go back to the salon

blocks.s: The beads in your fist seem to cool against your palm as you near a place the party's warmth does not reach, a servants' passage off the salon, narrow and stone and unlit, that the warded part of you can walk down where the comforted part of you would have drifted past without seeing. The cigarette smell of the dead coat is still on your hand and it goes ahead of you like a thin true thread.

The thin man is there, in the cold, alone, away from his door, as if he keeps a part of himself somewhere the house cannot warm. He looks at the beads in your hand and the practised face goes, for a moment, all the way off.

choices.label: Ask him what the cold passage is for

blocks.s: Up under the eaves a small window is lit a different colour, paler, bluer, the light of a room kept apart from the party's gold. A child stands in it. Small, in a nightgown, with both hands flat on the glass, looking down at you. A balloon hangs in the room behind, a birthday balloon gone soft and low on its string, the foil dull.
The child mouths something. Across the height and the rain you cannot hear it, but the shape of the mouth makes the same word twice. You think it is asking whether something is real.
Then a curtain you did not see move is drawn across, gently, from inside, and the pale square goes dark, and the warm gold of the party is all that is left.

choices.label: Go in to the door the thin man holds

blocks.s: You look back at the salon. It is genuinely lovely. That is the worst of it, that none of this is ugly. The fires, the kind faces turned toward you, the candle-tears, the music that will never resolve and so can never disappoint. A man near the fire raises his untouched glass to you in a small toast, welcome home, and means it, as far as he is able to mean anything any more.

At the edge of the firelight, just for a moment, you think you see someone wearing your own coat, standing the way you stand, the collar turned under wrong on one side. They are watching you decide. When you look straight at them there is only a coat-stand, and a coat, hung a little crooked.
The stair is still there. The one you came for is still down it.

choices.label: Turn your back on the warmth and step down
You cannot do it tonight; go back to the fire

blocks.s: You leave the photograph in your pocket. You cannot do it, not here, not crouched on a stranger's floor in the warm with the music never finishing. You stand up too fast and the room tilts gold around you and a woman puts a steadying hand on your arm.

Her hand is warm. Too warm, a degree past a living hand, the warmth of something kept by a fire that never goes out. "Careful," she says, kindly, glad. "You looked a long way off. Stay near the fire. Have something. You have had such a journey, and you are home now, and there is no hurry at all."
There is no hurry at all. The phrase settles over you, soft and total, and the stair at the far end of the room seems suddenly a very long way to make yourself walk.

choices.label: Let her lead you to the fire and a chair
Thank her, step back, and make yourself move

blocks.s: You stay in the warm dark of the porch a moment and let the house show you itself. Through the long windows the party moves: people in good clothes, holding glasses, leaning toward one another to laugh. They are beautiful and they are too still between movements, the way figures in an old painting are still, caught mid-gesture and never finishing it.

A woman by the fire lifts her glass to her mouth and does not drink and lowers it again. A man laughs with his head thrown back and holds it there a beat too long. Nobody is eating, though every surface carries food. Nobody is cold, though it is November and a window stands open to the rain, and not one candle gutters.
Then the thin man at the door turns his head and looks directly at you through the glass, as if you had knocked.

choices.label: Go to the door he is holding for you
Look up at the high windows where something pale is

35.title: The Man at the Cold Grate

blocks.s: Not every grate in the salon has a fire. At the one cold one, away from the warmth, a very large man sits on a low stool that is too small for him, elbows on his knees, and he is cracking one knuckle, slow, and counting under his breath. One. Two. He does not look at the party. He looks at the cold ash, as if it were a thing that needed watching.

"Far enough," he says, when you are still some way off, not unkind. "Three. I sit here because here I can do the maths. Closer to the warm and the maths gets hard. They have a use for me down there and the use is easy to fall into and I would rather. Four. Rather not. So I count."

choices.label: Ask him what use the house has for him
Leave him to his counting and go back to the salon

gated.aside: He cracks one knuckle and counts at the cold grate, and you feel your own hand answer, the same arithmetic of not-yet, not-yet. Two of a kind, holding the worst of yourselves off one number at a time.
Look how he holds it back. You know that grip. You have kept a thing past all reason yourself, white-knuckled, and the coven loves a keeper. It is the easiest hunger in the house to feed.

blocks.s: You try the handle. It is not locked. The girl says your name, or what passes for it, low and sharp, and does not stop you, because she has learned she cannot. The door opens a hand's width onto pale blue light, and there is the child, on the floor, the soft low balloon on its string tied to one wrist, both of you looking at each other through the gap.
"Is it real?" the child asks. Not afraid. Curious, the way the very young are curious about the only thing that frightens them.

You do not know which it the child means: the party, the keeping, the balloon, you. You suspect the child does not either, and that the not-knowing is the whole of the question. You ease the door shut before your hand can decide it belongs on that small head. The girl lets out a breath. "Yeah," she says. "That one. Now you've got it in you too."

choices.label: Go back down to the salon, carrying the question

gated.aside: Is it real. The question is already in your own mouth before the small voice asks it. You of anyone in this house know how it feels to be the thing nobody will let grow, or grieve.

blocks.s: You sit. It is, of course, the most comfortable chair you have ever sat in. The thing across from you lifts its head at last and it has your face, more or less, the way a photograph of you taken by a stranger is more or less your face, and it smiles with your mouth a half-beat slow.

"You sit, I go," it says, in something near your voice. "Or you go, and I sit, and I sit so well no one will ever know the difference, and the one downstairs will be kept, and kept, and kept, and you will never have to do the hard thing, because I will be here doing the easy one. Stay in the chair. Let me be the keeper. You have earned a rest. You of all people have earned a rest."
The fire is warm. The chair is warm. It is so reasonable, and the seat is moulded already to a shape that is almost exactly yours.

choices.label: Let it have your face and your place; stay in the chair
Get up out of the chair while you still can

blocks.s: The door is already open. It did not open just now; it has been open, you think, for a while, the warmth spilling out into the wet night without anyone minding the waste of it. A thin man in an old dark coat stands just inside, hands folded, the kind of stillness that comes from having stood a long time in one doorway.
A small dry click, a thumb on some catch in his sleeve, comes a half-second before he speaks.

"Good. This way. Mind the step; the stone sweats." His eyes pass over your face and something in them settles, a thing settling onto weight it has carried before. "They are expecting you. They have been expecting you for some time."

choices.label: Step over the threshold when he stands aside
Ask him whether you have met

blocks.s: "My use." Five. He cracks the knuckle again. "I'm big and I get hungry and the house knows both. Down at the table there's a job for a thing that's big and hungry and doesn't ask questions. Stand between the ones who want to leave and the door. Six." He says the next part to the ash. "Worst of it is it fits. I have wanted, in my life, to put my hands through a problem rather than around it. The house didn't make that. Just found it and built a stool for it by the cold grate so I'd have somewhere to sit and not do it."
He nods you off, the nod of a man staying put on purpose. "You're going down. Course you are. When you get there, mind the big quiet ones. Seven. We're the ones still deciding."

choices.label: Go back into the salon

blocks.s: You ask the voice who keeps the house, who poured the welcome over you at the door, who calls itself the Curator. The voice goes very quiet, and when it answers it does not finish your thought; it offers one of its own, which is rare enough that you go still to hear it.

"...older than the eldest here," it says. "Older than the table. The first of us, some say, the very first one who stood at a grave and could not, would not, walk away from it. The rest of us were kept. That one keeps. There is a difference, and there is not, and I have had a long time to fail to work out which." A breath that is not a breath. "It stages all our griefs so beautifully because it has never once looked at its own. Do not ask it a kind question. It will answer, and the answer will be a courtesy, and the courtesy will cost you."

choices.label: Ask it the way down
Go back to the salon

blocks.s: You stop on the warm side of the door and ask the thin man the only honest question you have: whether you can simply not do this. Whether a person can be un-welcomed. Whether the door works the other way.
He is quiet for a moment. The latch does not click; he has nothing to announce.

"For some," he says at last. "There is a way for some. It is not a door and it is not free and it is not tonight, and you are not ready to be shown it, because you have not yet seen what you came to see." He steps aside. "Refusing the warmth, here, only means standing in it cold. Go in. Standing on the step does no one any good, least of all the one downstairs."

choices.label: Go in to the salon
Refuse anyway; turn and walk back out into the night

blocks.s: You set the cup down and, because the wanting in the room is so strong and the only way you know to break a want is to say a truer one, you tell the watching party, plainly, who you came for and what you came to do.

The room flinches. Not the guests; the room. The candles lean. The music misses, for the first time all night, a beat it cannot then find again for a moment. Saying the want out loud cost the house something, and it cost you something too, because now they know, and the warmth that comes back is a degree more pointed, a welcome with an edge of appetite in it.
The servant withdraws with the full cup. You kept your edge and you spent some of your cover to do it. You feel, oddly, more yourself than you have since the porch.

choices.label: Go toward the stair down
Go back into the salon

blocks.s: The salon is the warmest room you have ever stood in, and the most crowded, and you have never felt more watched. Fires in two grates. Candles standing in their own slow tears. Velvet, and fur thrown over the velvet, and the guests moving among it all with their too-still faces and their full untouched glasses.

Nobody asks who you are. Several of them smile at you as you pass, warm, glad, the smiles of people pleased to see someone home. A wristwatch on the wrist of a man near the fire says ten past nine; you check it against the feeling in your own chest and the feeling says it has been ten past nine for a long time.
There is a stair at the far end of the room going down. There is so much, warm and bright, between you and it.

choices.label: Follow the unfinished music into the next room
Cross to the wall of portraits
Climb a few steps toward the humming you hear above
Find the cloakroom where the coats are kept
Take a cup from the tray a servant offers you
Notice the big man rationing himself by the cold grate
Try the small door behind the curtain
Let the hostess settle you on the long warm couch
Slip down the cold passage the beads seem to pull you toward
Go straight to the stair down and have done

blocks.s: You go down. The salon and its gold and its kind held faces close over behind you, and the warmth thins to memory, and the stair turns and turns again and keeps going, deeper than a house should reach, toward a low far light the colour of a single long-burning room and the smell, stronger with every step, of a feast laid out and never once cleared away.

You keep your hand off the wall and off the empty seat-shapes the dark keeps offering you. You came for one person. They are down here, somewhere under all this keeping, and tonight you are going to reach them.

Far below, deeper than the table, deeper than the feast, a single pair of hands claps once, slow, and stops, the way something claps once when it has seen a thing it means to judge later. Whatever it is down there, it has noticed you start down.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm, unhurried, from the stone itself:
There you go. Down to the table, where every place is laid and no one ever leaves hungry. Do take your time. We have so very much of it, and so, now, do you.

blocks.s: The stair lets you out into the long low light, and the table begins.

"Why don't I go down." She laughs, short, no fun in it. "Because down there everybody's having the loveliest time and nobody can leave and nobody will say so. I went down once. Somebody handed me a glass and called me darling and told me I'd never have to be afraid of anything ever again, and I realised that was a threat." She picks at the hem she hates. "I'm the newest. You can always tell the newest; we still flinch. Give it a hundred years, they tell me, and I'll stop flinching, and that's the part that scares me. I don't want to stop."
She hums the three notes once, fast, and they shake a little at the top, and you can hear that this is the sound she makes when she is afraid and refusing to show it.

choices.label: Ask what she wants, if not this
Ask about the closed door
Go back down to the salon

gated.aside: The three rising notes drift down the stair and you know them from the inside. That is the sound you make when you are afraid and will not say so. She is up there refusing to come down, and you understand the refusing all the way to the floor of you.

blocks.s: The hostess takes your arm, warm and certain, and steers you to a couch so long and so deep it could hold a whole tired family, and presses you down into it among the furs, and the heat of two fires reaches you from either side at once. "There," she says, glad, the way you are glad to get a guest finally off their feet. "You have done enough standing. You have done enough of everything. Let the house do it now."

The couch is exactly as wide as the wish to stop. You sink. The room blurs gold at the edges. Somewhere there was a stair and a cold and a reason, but the couch holds all of you and the fires warm all of you and the wanting drains out of your legs first and then higher.

choices.label: Tear yourself up off the couch before it finishes
Let it hold you; you have done enough

blocks.s: At the far end of the salon the floor gives way to a wide stone stair going down, and the warm gold light pours over its lip and does not go far, fading off into a lower dark that smells of cold stone and, faint, far below, of a long table laid and never cleared.

This is the edge of the comfort. You can feel it as a line across the room, the place where being looked after ends and the going-down begins. The house has no objection to your standing here. It would simply, sincerely, rather you turned around. There is a fire. There are friends who are so glad you are home. There is a chair with your name on the warm seat of it, and going down means leaving all of that for cold and for whatever it costs to reach the one you came for.

choices.label: Step down off the lip of the light
Look back at the warm room one more time
Slip into the warm side room you passed, just to rest first

blocks.s: You drink. It is the best thing you have ever tasted and the iron under the sweetness sits warm in your stomach and spreads, and a tightness you have carried so long you stopped feeling it as separate from yourself lets go, all at once, in your shoulders, your jaw, the back of your neck.

The room comes up brighter and kinder. The faces are not too still now; they are restful. The music is not unfinished; it is unhurried. You feel welcomed all the way down to the floor of yourself. A small voice that sounds like the humming girl says somewhere very far off that this is exactly the danger, and the small voice is easy, now, to set down.

choices.label: Take a second cup; one was so good
Set the warmth aside and remember why you came; go on

49.title: The Head of the Long Table

blocks.s: The stair lets you out at the head of a table so long you cannot find the end of it. It runs away from you down a low stone room into a dark that the candles do not reach, hundreds of places laid, every one of them set with good plate and a full glass and food that steams as if it had just been carried in, though no one is carrying anything and no one is eating.

The air down here is warm the way bathwater is warm when you have been in it too long, blood-heat, a degree past comfortable, and it carries the smell of the feast: roast and wine and hothouse flowers, and under all of it the sweet heavy note of meat kept a touch too long in a room that is never aired.

The seats nearest you are empty. One of them, at the head, on your right hand, is pulled out a little, turned toward you, waiting. Your hand starts toward the back of it before you have decided anything, to rest on the curve of a chair no one is sitting in. You take the hand back.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm, close, from the grain of the table itself:
Sit, sit. You have come such a long way down. There is always a place for you here. There has always been a place for you here, kept warm, against exactly this evening.

choices.label: Take the place that is pulled out for you
Stay on your feet and look down the length of the table
Look down at the pale thing moving under the table
Turn to the thin man who has come down the stair behind you

50.title: The Cup That Is Not Wine

blocks.s: A thrall steps in from the line of servers, smooth and worn and glad, and offers you a cup. It is not the wine they offered you upstairs. It is darker, and warmer, blood-warm, exactly the warmth of a living wrist, and the smell coming off it is all iron now and no cherries, no oak, no pretence, the honest smell of the thing the upstairs wine only hinted at.

"You have come so far," the thrall says, kind. "The stairs below are harder than the stairs above. This makes them nothing. This makes all of it nothing. One sip and the cold goes out of the going-down and you float to the bottom of the house light as a guest who never has to leave."
The cup steams faintly in the warm air. Somewhere under the wanting, a small dry voice that sounds like a girl on a landing says that floating to the bottom is exactly how you never come back up.

choices.label: Take the cup and drink the not-wine
Refuse it; you would rather feel the cold of the stairs
Hold up the beads between you and the cup

blocks.s: You drink, because you are so tired and the going-down is so long and the cup promised to make it nothing. It is warm all the way down and it does what it said. The cold goes out of you. The weight goes out of you. The hard clean line of yourself that you carried down the stair softens at the edges and you stop being able to find where it was.

The table is beautiful now. The thralls are restful, not worn. The guests are family. You feel the hunger of the house come into you and answer the hunger that was always in you, and the two of them shake hands and settle in like old friends, and a warmth opens in your chest that is the opposite of grief, a warmth that says no one is gone, no one is gone, no one will ever be gone again.
There was a reason you came down here. It is around somewhere. You will find it. There is no hurry. You let the thrall refill the cup.

choices.label: Drift on toward the foot of the table, fed and easy
Let the thrall seat you a moment; let the hunger settle in
Set the second cup down; try to find the thread you lost

blocks.s: You ask him how long he has sat on the too-small stool, counting. He does not answer in time, because he has no time to answer in; the clocks down here lie or stop.

"Long," he says. "The stool was bigger when they gave it me. Or I was smaller. Eight." He turns the knuckle without cracking it, a held thing held. "I came down hungry. Everyone comes down hungry; that is the whole of why anyone says yes. I thought the hunger would ease, kept. It does not ease. It only learns to wait, and I learned to wait with it, and now the waiting is the only me there is. Nine." He looks at his own hands as if they belonged to a larger man he used to be. "They put me at the door because I am the strongest. They do not know the strong part is not the arms. The strong part is the counting. Any night I stop counting I am exactly the thing they wanted when they sat me here, and the door does not need guarding then; it needs guarding from me."

He goes quiet. The number does not come. For a breath he is just a large tired man on a small cold stool, very far from a hunger he has spent a hundred years not feeding, and he looks at you as if your being able to leave were a thing he could almost remember wanting.

choices.label: Promise him you will go down slow, and mean it

blocks.s: You go through the iron door and the warmth of the table closes over behind you, and the cold of the lower house comes up to meet you, the good clean cold of stored things, of stone kept at one steady temperature for keeping's sake. The stair turns down and down, narrower now, and the smell of the feast thins behind you and a new smell takes it, older, drier, the smell of a place where the very oldest of them lie down and do not pretend to dine.

You carry the name still, behind your teeth, where it is heavy and safe. You carry whatever else you kept your hands on coming down. Below you, faint, far, a single slow clap sounds once in the dark, and stops, the way a thing claps once when it has seen something it intends to judge later.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm, unhurried, from the cold stone now as easily as from the warm:
Down to where the eldest sleep. Gently, gently. The ones below have been here longest, and they do not like to be hurried, and neither, soon, will you. We have so very much of it. Take the next step.

blocks.s: The stair lets you down toward the crypt, and the dark, and whatever has just clapped once and gone quiet.

54.title: Whether There Is a Way Out

blocks.s: You ask him the only practical question you have: whether a person who has come this far down can still go back up and out.
The latch does not click. He has nothing to announce.

"Up and out, tonight, with nothing seen and nothing chosen," he says at last, "yes. The stair behind you runs both ways still. It will not for much longer; the further down you go the more it forgets it was a way up. You could turn now, climb back through the warm, and walk out into the rain a tired living person who came to a terrible house and changed their mind. Many would call that the wise thing." The latch clicks, soft. "It is not the kind thing, for the one downstairs. But I have stood at doors a long time, and I have stopped being sure the wise thing and the kind thing are the same thing, or that you owe this house the kind one. The stair is there. I will not stop you on it either way."

choices.label: Decide to go on down; you did not come to turn around
Take the stair back up while it still remembers being a way up

blocks.s: You sit, because you have been on your feet a long time and the chair is exactly the height it should be and your knees did not ask your permission. The warmth comes up through the seat at once, the heat of a chair just vacated, though no one rose from it.

A glass is at your right hand. A plate steams in front of you. The places to either side fill, while you are not quite looking, with guests who lean in and are so glad you are here, who touch your sleeve, who tell you that you must be exhausted, that you must eat, that there is no hurry at all.

It would be easy to stay in this chair. That is the first true thing you have understood since the stair. The chair is not a trap because it is uncomfortable. It is a trap because it is the most comfortable thing in the world, and you have been so tired for so long.

choices.label: Make yourself rise again before the warmth settles into you
Stay seated; just take stock of the table from here

56.title: Too Far In to Recoil (an ending)

blocks.s: You try. Some last thread of the person who came down the stair holding a name like a coal pulls hard against the wrist in front of you and the lack of trouble around it, and for one clean second you are appalled, properly appalled, the appalled you should have been from the porch and were not, and you push the chair back to stand.

The chair does not move, because you do not, because the appalled second is only a second and the fed warmth is the rest of the night and all the nights after. You sit back down. The thread you pulled does not snap so much as soften, the way a wire softens in a flame until it simply is not a wire any more, until there is no holding it taut to push against.

The guest's cuff is still turned back. The wrist is still blue and patient and offered. You take what is offered, because everyone here does, because the cup told the truth and the going-down really is nothing now, and the reason you came down all this way is a kettle gone cold in a far room, and you are warm, and full, and home, and you will not, after all, be needing the iron door. A thrall turns the little name-card at your place face down. There is no hurry. There never was.

You tell him whatever it takes. You promise him slow and gentle and careful, and you do not mean a word of it; you mean only to get through the iron door and down to the one you came for and up the stair again as fast as a person can carry someone they love, and the Heavy, who has spent a long time learning the difference between a man still deciding and a man who has already decided to take, hears the lie under the words.

The counting stops. "Eight," he says, and there is no eight after it, no nine, no holding himself shut one number at a time, because you have just told him there is no point. "You already chose. I can hear it. I keep this door for the ones who would pull them up too fast and ruin it, and you are one, you just told me you are one." He stands, all of him, and he is not on the stool now and he is not counting now, and the part of him the house built the stool to keep sat down is, at last, given its turn.

He is sorry. You can see that he is sorry, in the half-second before. It does not stop him. The maths is over. The door stays shut, and you do not go down, and you do not go up, and the long table lays, in time, one more quiet place than it had before.

While you are at the table, a door at the side opens and two thralls carry a young woman through, between them, not roughly, the careful way you carry someone who has fainted and must be put somewhere soft. It is the girl from the landing, the newest-turned, the one who would not come down to the party.

She is not fainted. She is awake and furious and tired past furious, and as they carry her she hums, three rising notes, the same three, and the notes shake at the top, and it is plainly the sound she makes when she is afraid and will not give them the satisfaction of the scream.

"They keep re-seating me," she says to you, conversational, upside down over a thrall's shoulder, as if you had run into each other at a dull function. "I keep trying to stop. They keep starting me again. It's the rudest thing, honestly, not being allowed to be over." Then she is carried past, down the room, into the warm dark, and the three notes go on a while after you can no longer see her.

choices.label: Follow where they set her down before going on
Go on down the table, the notes still going

blocks.s: You ask it, as gently as you can, what its name was, before. It is a cruel question and a kind one at once, and you ask it anyway, because someone should, because no one in a hundred years has thought it worth the asking.

The thrall is quiet a long time. "...before," it says, and for once the word is not yours; it is reaching for its own. "There was a before. I can feel the shape of it, the way you feel a step that is not there in the dark and your foot goes down too far. There was a name. People put it in their mouths and meant me by it. A mother shaped it first. I have lost the sound and kept the weight of it, and the weight is the worst part, knowing there was a sound and not having it."

It steadies, flattens, becomes again the thing that finishes you. "This is what the keeping is for. Not the teeth; the teeth are quick. It is the long part after, where it takes the name, and then the wanting of the name, and then the part of you that could be sad it is gone. The eldest down there went first and went furthest. The very first of us could not bear a single grave, its own most of all, and so it built a house where no one need have one, and it has been so long forgetting that grave that it has made forgetting into the whole of the entertainment. It does not know its own name either. It only knows the show."

choices.label: Ask it the rules of keeping
Ask it who is the one kept furthest down

blocks.s: You shake your head and keep your hands at your sides. The thrall does not insist; it only waits, glad and patient, the cup steaming between you, certain you will change your mind, because everyone changes their mind, because the stairs below really are harder than the stairs above.

You would rather feel the cold of those stairs. The cold is the safe register here; you learned that upstairs, in the one cool cloakroom, in the servants' passage that smelled of a dead woman's cigarettes. The warmth is the threat. You want to arrive at the bottom of this house still able to feel how far down it is.

"As you like," the thrall says at last, withdrawing the cup, no edge in it, only the awful kindness. "It will be here. It is always here. You will pass it again on the way out, if you go out, and you will want it more then."

choices.label: Go on down to the foot of the table and the counting

blocks.s: Whether you run for the stair or stand your ground, it makes no difference, because the thing you named is faster than the running and louder than the standing. The name bound, the way the thrall said it would, and a name said in this house is not a call but a hook, and you have set it in the one you love and hauled.

They come up the length of the table toward you, fast, too fast, in the dark, and the candleflames bow flat as they pass, and the worst of it is that it is them, unmistakably them, the very person you came down all this way to reach, and you reach for them, of course you do, your arms going out for the shape you have ached toward for so long, and what arrives in your arms is warm and is them and is also, now, awake, and hungry, and bound to you by their true name, and so very, very glad, after all this keeping, to finally be reached.

You wanted them back. The house only ever wanted you to want it badly enough to do this. You hold each other at the head of the long table, the two of you, kept now together, and the feast lays a fresh place, and the Curator does not even need to speak, because you have done the whole of its work for it, with one word, said too soon.

The thin man from the front door has come down the stair at your back, quiet, his old dark coat the same down here as it was above, and a small dry click of some catch in his sleeve lands a half-beat before he speaks.

"This way down the room, when you are ready," he says. "Not before. Mind the step; the flags sweat down here, worse than the door." He does not sit. He never sits, you think; that is the whole of his discipline, a man who only ever stands in doorways. "I led you down to this table the night you said yes. I led the other one down past it. I have done this walk with a great many keepers and I will tell you the one thing I have learned, which is that the table is where most of them stop. They sit. They mean to go on. They are sitting still."
The latch clicks. "You came down on your feet. Stay on them as long as you can. The chair is patient. It can outwait anyone."

choices.label: Ask him plainly whether there is still a way back out
Thank him and go on down toward the flat voice in the corners

63.title: The Voice at the Table's Side

blocks.s: Halfway down the table, where the candlelight thins, a thrall stands against the wall in a servant's coat gone shiny at the elbows, so still and so long-kept that you took it at first for a coat-stand. It does not serve. It watches. When you come near it speaks, low and flat, and it speaks the end of your own thought a half-beat before you reach it.

"...the way down, yes," it says, as you think the way down. "You want past the table. Everyone who still wants anything wants past the table. I can tell you the way. I have stood here long enough to learn most things, including that telling is the only use I have left, which is more than most of them down there can say."
It does not move its hands. It has no glass, no plate, no place. It only stands and finishes other people's sentences, and has done, you think, for a very long time.

choices.label: Let it finish your words; ask it the way down plainly
Refuse to be finished; speak your own way through
Offer it a memory of yours in trade for the way down

blocks.s: You leave the chair where it is, turned toward you, patient, and you stay on your feet. A small cooling passes through the room, nothing you could name, the way a host's smile holds a half-second longer than it should when a guest declines to sit.

From your feet you can see the whole length of the table run down into the dark, and you can see that you are the only standing thing in it, which marks you, which is exactly what standing was going to cost. A guest near the head looks up at you, fond and a little puzzled, the look a kind person gives someone who insists on carrying their own bag.
"You'll tire yourself," she says, warm. "There is no need. Everyone sits, in the end."

choices.label: Walk on down the side of the table, still standing
Stop at the guests caught mid-gesture and look at them properly
Watch the two thralls carrying a struggling young woman past

blocks.s: You stop. You wait out the half-beat where it reaches ahead for your words, and you say nothing until the impulse to be finished passes, and then you ask, in your own plain voice, in words you chose, what the rules of this place are and where the way down lies.
The thrall goes quiet. You have done a small hard thing, holding your own sentence in your own mouth, and it has cost the thrall something to be refused the only office it has left.

"...you kept that one," it says at last, almost wondering, and for a moment it is not finishing you at all; it is saying a thing of its own. "Good. Keep doing that. It is the whole of the fight down here, keeping your own next word in your own mouth. I lost mine so long ago I cannot tell you my name. I look for it the way you look for a word on your tongue, and there is no tongue left to look with."

choices.label: Ask it the rules of keeping
Ask it gently what its name was, before
Ask it who is the one kept furthest down

blocks.s: You tell him you refused their cup upstairs, set it back full on the tray and kept your edge, the same way he sits at a cold door keeping his. Something moves in his big still face.

"You refused it." Eight. He cracks the knuckle once and lets the hand rest. "Most drink. I would drink, if I let myself stop counting; that is what the counting is for, the not-drinking. Nine." He looks at you, and for once there is no maths in the look, only one held man recognising another. "Then we are the same kind, you and me. The kind that is still deciding. I do not stop a man who is still deciding. I have too much respect for how hard it is."
He rises off the stool, slow, and moves his bulk away from the iron door, and the cold of the lower house comes up to meet you.

choices.label: Nod to him, one decider to another, and go down

blocks.s: You ask who is kept furthest down, at the centre of the house, the one you can feel like a low note under the music even here. The thrall is quiet for a long moment, and the candleflames lean toward you and away, and the warm air thickens.

"...the one you came for," it says, finishing you gently, and then, of its own, the thing you have walked down all this way not to be told: "You know who. You have known since the porch and made yourself not know it. The one at the centre was not taken from you. The one at the centre was turned, here, in this house, on a night you stood at the door and said the word that let it be done. You did not lose them. You kept them. They are down there warm and seated and exactly as alive-and-not as the night you said yes, and the reason they cannot leave is that someone has to keep the vigil, and the someone is you."
You put a hand out without deciding to, for the back of a chair, for a shoulder, for anything to hold, and your hand finds the cold edge of the table and nothing in the seat beside it.

choices.label: Speak the beloved's true name into the warm air
Hold the name behind your teeth; ask only the way on
Find you cannot bear what you just heard; turn for the stair up

gated.aside: You see it a moment before the room lets you: the one at the centre is not a stranger they took. The one at the centre is someone you put there.
You of all of them know the appetite to keep a thing because you decided it was real. That is the whole engine of this house, and you have felt it turn in your own chest.

blocks.s: You follow a little way and watch them set her down. They do not put her anywhere soft after all; they put her back in her place at the table, the same place she keeps leaving, and they tuck a napkin into her collar and straighten the cutlery she will not use and pour a glass she will not drink, and one of them winds the small travel clock at her place, a cheap brass thing with a cracked face, the kind you put by a bed, still keeping the time it kept the morning she was brought here.

She stops humming. She looks at the wound clock, and then at you, and there is nothing dry left in her face for a moment, only the tiredness under the jokes. "They wind it every day," she says. "My little clock. As if I had a morning to get up for. I keep trying to let it run down and they keep winding it." The three notes start again, low, going up, shaking at the top. "Tell whoever's down there for," she says, "that wanting to be over isn't the same as giving up. I'm not giving up. I'm just done. There's a difference and nobody in this house will let me have it."

choices.label: Carry that down the table with you

blocks.s: You bring the dead woman's beads up between you and the cup, the little cross hanging from your fist, worn smooth by hands that wanted comfort from something other than this house, and the thrall stops.

It does not recoil; the beads are not a weapon and this is not that kind of house. It simply cannot quite reach you across them, a kind word stopped at the edge of a person who has decided to keep grieving. The cup lowers. The glad face holds, but the gladness no longer lands on you; it slides off the cold edge the beads have put around you.

"You brought somebody's faith down here," the thrall says, almost wistful. "Somebody who would not be soothed. They walked out, I think. Very few do. Go on, then, you difficult thing. The big one at the door respects the difficult ones. He is difficult too."

choices.label: Go down to the foot of the table, beads in your fist

blocks.s: You take a breath of the over-warm air and take stock of yourself, patting your pockets at the door of all this. You have come a long way down with the worst of the comfort pulling at you, and you want to know what is still your own.

The tightness you came in carrying is still in your jaw and your shoulders, if you reach for it. If you kept your edge upstairs and would not be soothed by the cup, it is here, a hard clean line of yourself you did not let them loosen. If you took up the dead woman's beads in the cold cloakroom, they are still in your fist, warm now from your hand and cool again whenever the house leans too close.
Whatever you brought down, you have it still. Down here, you are going to need it.

choices.label: Go down the table toward the flat voice in the corners

blocks.s: You do not argue and you do not ask. You lower yourself onto the cold flags across from him, with your back to the warm table and your face to the iron door, and you wait, and you let him count.

He glances at you once, then back to the door. "Five," he says. "You are not talking." Six. "Most talk. They want past so bad they fill the air. You are just sitting." Seven. The numbers come slow, and you match your breath to them without deciding to, and the cold of the floor comes up through you, clean, the safe register, the opposite of the warm that is at your back. "Eight. I count to keep my hands. You know that. You are counting with me now." Nine. "That is the first kind thing anyone has done at this door in a long time. Sat in the cold and counted with the man, instead of around him."

For a while there are no numbers, only the two of you and the breathing and the cold, and then he says, very quietly, not a count at all, "Ten," and gets up off the too-small stool, and stands aside, and the way down is open, and he does not look at you go, because a man holding himself shut does not need to be thanked for it and would rather not be watched.

choices.label: Get up off the cold floor and go down through the iron door

blocks.s: You let it. It is easier to let it; the words come out of the corner a half-beat ahead of you and you only have to agree, and there is a terrible rest in that, in not having to find your own sentences in a room this warm.

"...past the table," it says, for you, "down to where the eldest sleep, and below them the cellar, and below that the room you came for. The house lets you go down. I told you that already, or you did, I no longer always know which of us is speaking, and neither, after a while, will you." A flat evenness, no fear in it because fear has been kept out of it for too long. "That is the first rule of keeping. It does not take you all at once. It takes the seams between you and the next person, and then there is no you to take."

choices.label: Ask it the rules of keeping
Ask it who is the one kept furthest down

blocks.s: You stay seated, just to take stock, just for a moment. From the chair the table is even longer, and you can see now what you could not from your feet: the guests nearest you are bright and lively, the ones a little further down are slower, and the ones at the dim middle distance barely move at all, lifting a fork an inch and setting it back, turning a head a degree, worn smooth as old coins by the same evening repeated without end.

The warmth is in your shoulders now. The glass is in your hand; you do not remember picking it up. A guest beside you is mid-sentence about a summer you both apparently remember, fondly, though you have never met, and it would be so rude to stand up now.
You put the glass down. The small effort of putting it down tells you how far in you already are.

choices.label: Stand up out of the chair while standing is still yours to do
Let the guest finish their story; lean back into the warm

blocks.s: You set the second cup down and try to find the thread, the reason, the hard thing you came down all this way to do. It was here a moment ago. There was a stair. There was a name you were holding behind your teeth, careful, like a coal, and the not-wine has gone through you warm and even and you cannot now find the coal or the teeth or the holding.

There was a person. At the bottom of the house. You came for them. You are almost sure. The feeling of them is still there, a low note under the music, but it does not hurt any more, and a thing you came to do that does not hurt is a thing you will get to later, and there is so much later down here, an endless warm forever of later.

You drift toward the foot of the table, fed and easy, and the iron door, and the big man counting, and when he says far enough you simply smile at him, glad, no want left in you for him to bar, and you turn back to the warm and the laid plates and the family, and you take, after all, the place that was pulled out for you, because there is no longer any reason in the whole warm world not to sit.

75.title: The Stair That Still Remembers (an ending)

blocks.s: You take the stair back up while it still remembers being a way up, and the thin man does not stop you; he only inclines his head, and the latch clicks once, and you go, past the table and its laid forever, past the warm salon and the kind glad faces, out under the warm portico and down the soft wet gravel into the honest November cold.

This time the drive ends. The gate is where you left it. You came to a terrible house and you changed your mind, and you walk away a tired living person, aging, mortal, free, and you tell yourself, for years, that it was the wise thing, that no one could have asked you to go down into that and come back able to do what going down would have asked.

It is the wise thing. The thin man said so himself. He also said he had stopped being sure the wise thing and the kind thing were the same, and on the bad nights, the ones near anniversaries, you hear that in his old flat voice, and you go to the window, and you do not, of course, see the house, because it is not on any road you can drive. It is only down. You never went down. The one you came for is still there, kept, warm, uncome-for, and you are out here, safe, where people are allowed to grieve, grieving the wrong thing, which is your own wisdom.

You promise him you will go down slow, that you will not pull the one you love up out of their keeping before you understand what reaching them costs, and you find as you say it that you mean it, that holding the name behind your teeth this whole way down was the start of meaning it.
The Heavy listens the way a large man listens, with his whole body, and then he nods once and begins, deliberately, to count to ten.

"One," he says, and stands. "Two. Three." He steps off to the side of the iron door, and the cold of the way down spills across the sill. "Four. Five. Go now, while I am counting and not deciding. Six. Mind the step; it is wet, and it is a long way, and the table was only the start of the way down. Seven." You go past him, and behind you the slow count goes on, a big broken man holding himself shut one number at a time so that you can pass.

choices.label: Go down through the iron door

blocks.s: "The rules," the thrall says, flat, certain. "There are not many. Nothing here dies, so nothing here may be grieved, because grief admits a death, and we admit none. That is the only rule and it is every rule. You may not say goodbye in this house. You may not light a candle for anyone. You may not let a place at the table go empty and call it loss; we leave the empty places set and call them waiting."

A breath that is not a breath. "The keeping feels like mercy from the inside. No one you love is ever gone. That is exactly the trap. You will be offered, before the night is done, the most comfortable thing a person can be offered, which is that you never have to lose anyone again. Down here we have all taken it. Look at us. We are what the offer makes."

choices.label: Ask who is kept at the very bottom of the house
Go still at a single slow clap from somewhere far below

blocks.s: Something pale moves at the edge of your eye, low down, under the table among the chair-legs, and when you look there is a child there, sat cross-legged on the cold flags in the warm room, a birthday balloon gone soft and low tied to one wrist, the foil dull, the string slack.

The child is not eating either. There is a high chair at the table above, you notice, a small carved chair set with a small plate and a small cut-crystal glass, the glass holding juice that has gone to dark syrup at the bottom from sitting full and undrunk for longer than juice should sit. The chair is never used. The child sits under it instead, where it can watch the legs of the grown dead go by.

"Is it real?" the child asks you, around the slack string, not afraid, only curious, the way the very young are curious about the only thing that frightens them. You do not know which it the child means, and you suspect the child does not either.

choices.label: Ease back from the table before your hand decides to reach down

blocks.s: You bring up the beads and let him see the little cross worn smooth in your fist. The Heavy looks at it a long moment, and the counting slows.

"Eight," he says, very quiet, as if the number were a name he half-recognised. "That belonged to somebody who would not be soothed. I remember her. I was new then. They wanted me to stop her at a door like this one and I counted and counted and could not make myself, and she walked out, and it is the one good thing I have done in this house, the doing of nothing." He cracks the knuckle, slow. "You carry her faith. I will do the one good thing again."
He stands, all of him, and steps off to the side of the iron door, and the cold of the way down pours unblocked across the threshold.

choices.label: Thank him, and go down warded

80.title: The Feast Closes Over You (an ending)

blocks.s: You lean back into the warm and let the guest finish the story about the summer you never had together, and it is a good story, and there is another after it, and another, and you find that the seat has learned your shape and you cannot now feel where the chair ends and you begin.

No one ever tells you to stop. That is the whole of it. You have been carrying a hard thing alone for so long, and down here the table is laid forever and the food steams and is never eaten and the glasses fill and are never drained and no one is ever gone, no one, no one will ever be gone again, and somewhere far below the one you came for sits warm and kept and uncome-for, waiting for a keeper who has sat down two floors above them and will not now be getting up.

A thrall turns the little card at your place so the name faces down, the card turned for a guest who has settled and will not rise. You do not see them do it. You have stopped seeing most things. You pass the dish. There is no hurry. There is no hurry at all.
You ask him why he keeps a door he so plainly hates keeping. The counting stops, which you did not expect.

"Because if I am here, counting," he says, "I am not down there, doing. Five. The house wants me to be the thing that puts its hands through people. I keep finding out it is right, that I am partly that, and partly I am the man who counts so the first part never gets its turn. Six." He looks at you for the first time. "You came down with the name of somebody in your mouth and you did not say it. I can tell. The ones who say it I have to stop, because they come up the stair too fast and wrong. You held it. Seven."

He shifts on the too-small stool, and the iron door is, for a moment, not quite so fully blocked. "Tell me you will go down slow. Tell me you will not pull them. Say it and I will count to ten instead of three and let the door alone while I do."

choices.label: Promise him you will go down slow, and mean it
Ask him how long he has sat counting at this door
Lie; tell him whatever gets you through the door fastest

blocks.s: You offer it a trade, because it has nothing and you have your whole life behind you, more than you can carry down a stair like this. You give it a memory you can spare: an ordinary afternoon, a kitchen, the radio on, nothing in it but being alive and not knowing it was precious.

The thrall takes it the way a dry sponge takes water, and for a moment, just a moment, it is somebody. Its flat face moves. "...a kitchen," it says, and it is not finishing you; it is remembering being finished, being whole, being a person with a kitchen of its own. "Thank you. I had one. I think. They keep you so long the kitchen goes, and the radio, and then the wanting of them." It steadies. "For that, I will tell you true, and I will tell you the part the house would rather I left out."

choices.label: Hear the rules of keeping
Ask straight out who is kept at the centre
