blocks.s: From somewhere far down, below the table, below the iron door, a single slow clap sounds once and stops. Just one. The thrall flinches, which is the most a thing this worn can do.

"...the eldest," it says, finishing the question you have not yet asked. "That is the one who decides who is worth keeping. It claps when it has seen something it means to judge later. It clapped once for you upstairs; I heard it through the floor. It has clapped again now. I would not flatter myself, if I were you. It claps for the meat and for the keeper with the same two hands." A flat pause. "Whatever you do down there, you will do it in front of it. It does not interfere. It only watches, and decides, and one day, far off, delivers its verdict, and by then you will have forgotten you were ever on trial."

choices.label: Ask who is kept at the very bottom of the house

blocks.s: You say it. The name. The true one, the one only you and a handful of the dead ever used, the name that means a person and not a fact, and it comes out of you before your sense can stop it, because you have not said it aloud in so long and the room is so warm and the thrall has just told you they are down there to be reached.

The table hears it. Not the guests; the table. The candleflames stand straight up. A long shiver runs down the laid plates, glass touching glass, all the way into the dark where you cannot see, and from very far below comes an answer, a single low note that is almost your name said back in a voice you would know anywhere, rising, getting nearer, getting faster.
The thrall's flat voice, for once, is afraid. "...you named them," it says. "A name binds, down here. You did not call them. You pulled them. And things that are pulled come up too fast."

choices.label: Run for the cellar stair before the rising thing reaches you
Stand your ground and wait for what your own voice raised

blocks.s: The table ends at last at a low arched door of black iron, set into the stone, and the cold comes off it in a clean line you can feel from ten feet away, the cold of the way down. In front of the door, on a stool too small for him, sits a very large man, elbows on his knees, cracking one knuckle, slow, counting under his breath.
One. Two. He does not look at you. He looks at the iron door, the way a man looks at a thing he is keeping himself from doing.

"Far enough," he says, not unkind. "Three. They put me here to keep the wanting ones from the door. You want the door. Four. So." He cracks the knuckle. "We have a problem, you and me, and I would rather solve it with words, because the other way I do not get to stop once I start."

choices.label: Talk to him; ask why he keeps a door he hates keeping
Say nothing; sit on the cold floor and wait out his count with him
Show him the beads; ask warded passage
Tell him you refused their cup, the same as he refuses the warm

blocks.s: You stop at the bright end of the table and make yourself look at the guests, full on, which you would not let yourself do upstairs. Up close the liveliness comes apart. A man is half-risen to propose a toast, the glass at the top of its lift, his mouth open on the first word, and he has been there long enough that a film has come up over the wine in the held glass and the wine has gone the brown of old tea. A woman has turned to lay her hand on his sleeve and the hand never quite lands; it hovers a hair above the cloth, forever about to be affectionate.

Nearest you a man wears a good watch, and the watch is going, the second hand sweeping, reading ten past nine, the only moving thing at his place. He died at ten past nine, you think, on some night a long time ago, and his watch has not yet been told. A wedding ring sits loose on a finger gone too thin for it.

None of them sees you. They are not waiting for the toast to land. The toast already landed, a hundred years of evenings ago, and they are still inside the half-second after, where nobody has to say what comes next.

choices.label: Step back from the toast that will never finish

blocks.s: The name comes up your throat and you hold it. You keep it behind your teeth, where it has lived for longer than this house has had a word for time, because the thrall has just told you a name binds down here, and you did not come this far to do the one thing the house would most like you to do, which is to pull the one you love up out of their keeping before you understand what reaching them will cost.

The thrall watches you hold it. "...good," it says, finishing the breath you do not let out. "You wanted to. Everyone wants to. The ones who say it are kindest and arrive soonest and ruin it. You will reach them. Reach them slowly. The way on is past the foot of the table, where the big one counts at the cellar door."
You keep the name. It is heavier, held, than it would be spent, and you carry it on down the table like a coal in a cold hand.

choices.label: Pass a thrall who offers you the cup that is not wine
Notice someone at the table's foot wearing your own coat
Go straight down to the foot of the table and the counting

blocks.s: The thrall draws out a chair for you, the one chair you swore upstairs you would not take, and now that the cold has gone out of you there is no reason not to, so you sit, fed and easy, and you let the hunger settle the rest of the way in.

It is not your hunger. That is the part the cup did not warn you of. It comes up from the floor of the house and finds the small old hunger that lives in everyone, the one that wants nobody to ever leave, and it marries it, and the wedding is warm and total and you cannot now tell the borrowed appetite from your own. A guest beside you turns back her cuff, friendly, offering the blue inside of her wrist the way another night a stranger might offer you a cigarette, and you find you are looking at it, and that the looking does not trouble you, and that the lack of trouble is the most frightening thing left in you that can still feel frightened.

Far off, below the iron door, a name you used to be holding goes quiet, like a kettle taken off the heat. You do not notice it go. There is a small carved high chair across the table with a glass of juice gone to dark syrup, untouched, set for a child who never comes, and you look at the syrup and feel nothing about it at all, and that is the last of the keeping done.

choices.label: Rise, full and untroubled, and drift to the iron door
Try, while a thread of you is left, to recoil from your own wrist-looking

blocks.s: At the far foot of the table, in the last of the candlelight before the iron door, someone is sitting in your coat. Not a coat like yours; your coat, the same wear at the cuffs, the same missing second button, and the collar is turned under wrong on the one side you would never turn it.

They are practising you. They lift a glass the way you lift a glass and set it down a half-beat slow, like a person learning a part from a recording. They do not look up as you come near. They are too busy being you to notice you, which is, you think, the most unsettling thing you have seen in a house full of unsettling things.

"Not yet," they say, in a voice almost your voice, still to the glass. "You haven't been all the way down yet. I don't make my offer until you've seen the bottom. Go on. I'll keep your seat warm. I'm good at that. It's the only thing I'm for."

choices.label: Leave it to its rehearsal and go on to the counting man

blocks.s: You cannot bear it. The thrall has just told you the thing you walked down all this way refusing to know, that the one at the centre was not taken from you but kept by you, turned on a night you stood at a door and said the word, and the weight of it folds your knees, and you turn, and you go back up the table toward the stair, fast, not running, a person walking very deliberately away from a fire.

You keep the comfort. That is the trade you make on the stair, without quite letting yourself name it: you climb back up into the warm, into the laid feast and the kind faces and the no-one-is-ever-gone, and you stay, because to go down and reach them would mean facing what reaching them costs, and you have just learned what you did, and you cannot, tonight, learn what it would take to undo it.

The house is so glad to have you back from the cold edge of the table. A chair is pulled out. A glass is filled. You took the worst news a person can take and you brought it up into a room that does not permit grief, and so you do not grieve, you cannot, you only sit, warm, with the knowledge sitting warm and undealt-with inside you, forever, which is exactly how the house likes its keepers: informed, and comfortable, and still.

The cellar stair lets you out into the first cold you have felt since the rain. Not the dead frost you half-remember from some other house; the plain stone cool of a place dug down under a warm one and kept cold on purpose. After all that gold and hearth-heat your skin is grateful for it, and that gratitude is the first honest thing the house has let you feel since the door.

This is the crypt. Long low vaults running off into the dark, and in them the eldest, laid out on stone shelves, sleeping the way the deep dead are supposed to and the upstairs guests are not. A single oil lamp burns on a bracket. Nothing here is perfumed. Nothing here pretends.
Something moves at the edge of the lamplight, wearing your coat, and the collar is turned under wrong on the side you would never turn it.

choices.label: Walk the vaults first; look at the eldest before anything looks at you
Take stock of what you still have on you before it speaks
Step toward the figure in your coat

blocks.s: You lift the latch yourself. It clicks under your thumb, the same small dry click that goes ahead of the thin man's voice, and the wedge of black widens to a doorway, and the wet November night comes in cold and real and ordinary, rain and gravel and the smell of the living world where things are allowed to end.

You step through. The thin man does not stop you. He holds the door, which is his whole job, and inclines his head as you pass, and says, "Mind the step," and the step is the lip of a coal hatch onto wet grass, and then you are out, in the rain, on the grounds, alive.

But you went out alone. That is the thing the door cannot fix. You walked down all those warm stairs and all those cold ones to reach the one you came for, and you stood one short stair above them, and then you turned and lifted a latch and saved only yourself. The gate is where you left it. The drive ends. You go free, aging, mortal, into the rest of your life, and you spend it knowing exactly where they are, kept, warm, uncome-for, the last cold stair never walked, because when the door finally opened you found it was easier to be the one who left than the one who stayed to carry it down. The house did not keep you. You only kept yourself from finishing what you came to do.
You take the second door. You give it your face.

It is the easiest thing you have ever done, which is how you know it is the worst. You hand over the name and the bad knee and the creased photograph against your chest and the whole shabby remainder of your mortal life, and with it you hand over the part of you that came down all these stairs on purpose, the chooser, the keeper, the one who flinches. The thing in your coat puts your face on properly at last, and the collar rights itself, and it goes up and out into the rain to live your life so badly that no one out there will ever know the difference, least of all you.

Because you stay. You go back up to the gold, and they are so glad you are home, and someone presses a warm cup into your hand and you drink it without a thought because you no longer have the part that would have thought, and you take a seat by the fire among the too-still faces and you smile your own too-still smile, and you never decide anything again. Not the chair. Not the vigil. Not even your own ending. Far below, the one you came for is kept by no one who remembers why they were worth coming for, slipping a little smoother every year, and you do not think of them, because you gave away the part of you that could. You wanted, on the porch, in the rain, to set the whole thing down. You finally have. You will never pick anything up again.

You let the seam decide, and you trust it to tell you the copy from the real thing, except that you never actually looked for the seam, never named the collar or the half-beat smile, and so when the Understudy says, gently, in your exact voice, that of course it is the real one, that you are the copy, that you are the want it made a body of and it is the man who walked down all these stairs, you have nothing to set against it. No flinch you tested. No collar you checked. Only its calm, certain, loving face, which is your face, telling you who you are.

It is so reasonable. You are so tired. And it knows things, your things, the Tuesday, the photograph, the night on the drive, because it has studied you down to the floor, and a thing that knows your whole life sounds exactly like the one who lived it.

You step back to let the real one pass. You take its place on the stone shelf among the sleeping elders, to wait, to be the spare, the understudy, the want without a body, while it straightens a collar that was never wrong and goes down to do the hard thing you came to do, wearing the face you have somehow agreed was always its. Some long time later, in the cold, you try to remember the shape of your own mouth, and find you cannot, and decide it does not matter, because you are only the copy, and a copy does not need a mouth of its own.
"Two things," it says. "I will lay them both down and you can pick over them.

"One. I take your seat. The one waiting at the centre, the keeper's place beside the one you came for. I sit it instead of you, forever, and you climb back up that stair and out the far door into the rain, and you grow old, and you die the ordinary way, free. The one downstairs stays kept, exactly as they are, and I keep them, and you never have to be the one who decided.

"Two. You give me your face, all of it, not just the loan I have now, and I become you, out there, in the daylight, living the rest of your little mortal life for you so you never have to feel it run out. And you stay here, in the warm, kept, choosing nothing, ever. No grief. No ending. No more of this awful arithmetic you do behind your eyes."
It spreads your hands, palms up, the gesture you make when you are being reasonable. "Take-my-seat, or wear-my-face. Either way the weight comes off you. That is the whole of what I am for."

choices.label: Press it on the first door: take-my-seat
Press it on the second door: wear-my-face
Watch its face instead of listening; look for the seam
Heed the flat voice that has begun saying your next words for you
Ask it the only question that matters: what it gets out of this

gated.aside: Both doors it holds out lead to the same room. One asks you to leave the cost behind. One asks you to stop being the one who pays it. Neither lets the one at the centre go.

blocks.s: You stop listening to the words and watch the face make them. It is good. It is very good. But you have spent your whole life inside that face and it has spent, what, weeks, and there is a seam. The smile lands a half-beat after the eyes. When it says the one you came for it does not flinch, and you always flinch, a small private flinch behind the teeth, every single time, and it does not have that yet because it has never had to mean it.

The collar is the tell that gives the rest away. Turned under wrong on the one side, the side you would never turn, because it learned you from the outside, from photographs and from watching, and it has never once stood in front of a mirror as you and hated what it saw.

choices.label: Name the seam out loud; tell it you can always see when it lies
Say nothing; keep the seam to yourself as a weapon

blocks.s: You turn the card over on it. You make it hold the child the way you have to hold her, not the warm version it is selling but the true one. A girl who was promised a birthday that never ends and got a birthday that never ends. The balloon that will never be cried over because it can never quite finish going down. The same age, the same nightgown, the same blue light, for two hundred years and two hundred more, asking through the glass whether it is real, because some part of her still young enough to ask already knows the answer is no.

"You say take your seat and the child stays warm," you tell it. "She is not warm. She is kept. You have seen the eldest on the shelves; that is where warm goes. You are not offering to spare her an ending. You are offering to spare me the sight of giving her one. Those are not the same mercy, and you know it, because you are the part of me that knows it and wishes I did not."

The Understudy is quiet. Far above, the balloon taps the door once, and stops. "Yes," it says at last, your voice with all the salesmanship gone out of it. "That is true. I told you I meant every word, and I did, and that one is the truest and the cruelest. I cannot use her on you if you will insist on seeing her. Very few can stand to. That is why she works."

choices.label: Refuse the trade; go down to give the hard thing honestly
Refuse it knowing the door is real, the child clear in your mind

98.title: Because It Is the Part That Wanted To

blocks.s: You ask it how it can be so sure you would take a thing this terrible, walking out free while it sat your forever in the cold. It does not smile this time. The borrowed face goes very plain, plainer than yours ever manages in a mirror, because it has nothing to perform for itself.

"Because I am the part of you that already decided," it says. "On the porch, in the rain, before you ever knocked. There was a half-second up there, you will not remember letting yourself have it, when you thought: I could turn around. I could let it be over. I could go home and grieve like an ordinary man and sleep through a whole night again. That half-second is me. The house found it and gave it a coat and a face, your coat, your face, and set it down here to wait for you, because the house knows that the part of a man that wants to stop is the easiest part to do business with." It looks at you, almost tender. "I am not tempting you with something foreign. I am only your own quietest wish, said out loud where you have to hear it. That is why it works. That is the whole trick, and I am telling it to you, and it will still work."

choices.label: Ask what it costs IT to sit that chair forever
Go find the Usher before you answer your own quietest wish
Go to the choosing

blocks.s: You walk the vaults before you let the thing in your coat have you. The lamp throws your shadow long ahead and the cold deepens with every step away from the bracket, an honest cold, a basement cold, the kind that means stone and depth and nothing else.

The eldest lie in shelved niches cut into the rock, one above another like berths on a ship that is never going anywhere. They are not corpses; corpses you could have borne. They are the warm upstairs guests run all the way out, the keeping taken to its end. The oldest faces have gone smooth, the features worn down to a suggestion, a thumb of features pressed nearly flat. They are not dead. You can see, if you look, the smallest rise and fall, slowed almost to nothing, a breath every long minute or two, patient as the cold.

On the lip of one niche, set down by some hand a hundred years ago and never picked up again, a man's reading spectacles wait, one arm folded, one open, exactly where he left them the last evening he meant to read.

choices.label: Look closer at the spectacles, the thing that is only a thing
Read the names cut over the niches
You have seen enough of the cold; go to the figure in your coat

gated.aside: The eldest inclines its head to you, one judge to another. You know this hunger, the pencil poised before the verdict, the keeping of what you have ruled worth keeping. The recognition is the danger.

blocks.s: You say nothing about the seam. You let it think its face is perfect. There is a cold comfort in knowing a thing your enemy does not know you know, and after a night of being known down to the floor of yourself by everyone in this house, the comfort of holding one card face-down is enormous.

It keeps talking, easy, confident, and you keep nodding, and the whole time you are watching the half-beat smile arrive late and thinking: I have you. Whatever this becomes, I have one true thing you do not, and a true thing is the only currency that buys anything in a house that deals in comfortable lies.

choices.label: Go find the Usher; the seam is no use if the door is a wall
Go straight to the choosing, knife behind your back

blocks.s: You tell it no. You do not know, for certain, that any door out exists; nobody has shown you a way that was free and tonight, and so refusing is not the clever move or the safe move, it is only the true one. You will not hand your weight to a copy of yourself so a person made of your worst tiredness can carry it forever. If it has to be carried, you will carry it, down, to wherever the carrying ends.

You find you are refusing it the only way you have seen refusal actually done in this house, which is the big man's way at the cold grate: not in one grand no but in a held one, the chair pulling at your hand and your hand not going, one number at a time. The Understudy is the warm easy thing in front of you, the same warm easy thing the hunger was in front of him, and you keep your hands shut on nothing the way he kept his. One. Two. Rather not.
The Understudy takes it well, because it is, in the end, you, and you have always taken no better than yes.

"Then go down," it says, your voice gone quiet. "Carry it. I will be here, being the part of you that wishes you would not." From somewhere in the dark behind the sleeping elders, slow and deliberate, a single pair of hands begins to clap, unhurried, weighing you, and somewhere far below three rising notes go up and do not quite reach rest, and you go on down.

choices.label: Go down the last cold stair to the cellar

blocks.s: You let the verdict in. The eldest wants you, and it is the oldest thing in the house, and there is a part of a tired man that has waited his whole life to be told by something that he is worth keeping, and that part of you lifts its head at the word like a dog at its name.

You do not take the Understudy's seat and you do not wear its face. You do something quieter and worse. You stop, here, in the cold, halfway down, and you decide not to decide, not tonight, not for a long time. You will go back up to the warm a while, the eldest says, and think it over, and come down again when you are ready, and the swept shelf at your height will keep, because everything down here keeps, that is the whole point of the place.

The clapping resumes, slow and satisfied, and the pencil makes its calm record, and the Understudy in your coat watches you choose the one outcome it never offered, because it never had to. You came down to refuse a copy of your own wish and you have ended by agreeing with a stranger's appraisal of you instead. The one you came for waits one short stair below, uncome-for, while you go back up to be told again how worth keeping you are, and the centuries the eldest promised begin, unhurried, the way everything begins in this house, with no hurry at all and a chair always just refilled.
You sink down at the far cold end of the vault, where the honest draught comes through, and you do not get up.

The warmth you drank at the table is in you still, and it does not want the cold, and it does not want the rain, and it does not, in the end, want anything but to be left alone in the comfortable dark. The thin man stands his post a little way off, and the latch is right there, just a latch, never locked, working both ways for anyone able to want it, and you find you are no longer able. You can see the wedge of wet night. You simply cannot make yourself rise toward it. The cup is stronger than the door. That was always the point.

You settle. The eldest sleep on their shelves around you and you become, by slow degrees, another shape in the cold that the house no longer needs to watch, because a thing that wants nothing makes a poor escape risk. The one you came for stays kept, far below, uncome-for the last short stair. You came so close. You came all the way down to the cold honest air, and the cup walked you the last few steps back into the keeping, gently, the way it does everything, with no hurry at all.

You pick them up. Steel rims gone soft grey with age, the little screws at the hinges seized solid. One lens has a hairline crack across the corner, the kind you keep meaning to get seen to and never do, because they still work, because there is always next month. The man who set them down here was waiting for next month two hundred years ago, and he is in the niche above them still, breathing his one slow breath a minute, and the spectacles have outlasted the eyes they were ground for, and they will outlast yours.

That is the thing the crypt does that the gold upstairs cannot. It does not argue. It does not soothe. It simply holds up, in the honest cold, what keeping comes to in the end: a smooth blank face and a pair of glasses no one will ever need, kept clean of dust by no one, going nowhere, forever. Tucked into the same niche, half under the cold lip of the stone, is a folded racing form from some town you have never heard of, a horse called Better Late ringed twice in soft pencil, the race run and settled longer ago than the spectacles or the man or anyone now alive could say. Whether the horse came in, there is no telling, and no one to ask, and it does not matter, and you find you would still like to know. You leave it where it lies.
You set the spectacles back exactly as you found them, one arm folded, one open. It seems wrong to leave them any other way.

choices.label: Read the names cut over the niches
Go to the figure in your coat, colder now and clearer

blocks.s: You leave the Understudy in your coat among the eldest and you go down the last stair, the coldest one, the stone wet now with the same real cold as the draught at the far door, and the smell changes from old stone to something else, something low and red and living, the smell of a cellar where the house keeps not its wine but the thing under the wine.

You did not take the easy trade. You could have. A part of you, a whole walking talking part of you, offered to lift it off your shoulders and you said no, and the saying of it is the heaviest thing you have done since the night you said yes on the drive. You carry that down too.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm, courteous, from the cold stone, a touch too kind:

You turned down a very generous offer. We were watching. We are always watching, but tonight we were watching the way one watches someone we have decided to keep. Down you go, then, to the cellar, and the one who turned them, and the truth of what keeping costs. Mind the step. There is no hurry. There is only, ever, down.

blocks.s: The stair lets you out into a low red dark, and the cellar begins.

You tell it no, and you tell it the way only a person who has seen the open door can tell it. You know the latch works. You know you could be in the rain inside a minute, old and free and grieving nobody. You are turning that down on purpose, with your eyes open, because the thing it offers to take off your shoulders is the only thing down here that was ever yours to carry.

It nods, your nod, slow. It is not surprised and it is not, quite, disappointed; it is something harder to look at, which is proud of you and sorry for you in the same expression, the expression you make at people you love who are about to do something brave and stupid.

"Of course," it says. "You saw the door and you are walking past it. You would. You are the part that keeps walking." From the dark behind it, slow and deliberate, comes the sound of a single pair of hands beginning to clap, unhurried, appraising, approving, the slow clap of something very old that has decided you are worth watching after all.

choices.label: Go down the last cold stair to the cellar

blocks.s: "The draught," he says, "is the only honest air in the house, and it comes from there." He sets his hand flat on a low iron door in the stone you would never have found, no bigger than a coal hatch, frosted at its edges with real cold. "The far door. The one I told you about upstairs, the one I opened once for a woman who would not be soothed and walked out with her husband in a box and her whole mortal grief ahead of her."

He works the catch. It is just a latch. A plain iron latch, the kind on any garden gate, and it lifts under his thumb with the same small dry click that goes ahead of his voice, and a wedge of black wet night shows through, rain, gravel, the ordinary cold world where things end.

"It was never locked," he says. "That is the part they do not want you to see. It opens from the inside with one thumb. The house does not keep you with a lock. It keeps you with the warm, and the warm is so much stronger than a lock. But the latch is just a latch. It works both ways. I am showing you because you came down cold and would not be soothed, and that is the only kind of person it is any use to show."

choices.label: Ask him why he does not simply walk out himself
Open it now and walk out into the rain
Close it; you are not leaving without the one you came for

gated.aside: He shows you the latch and his tired face eases, because for once he is not the only one in the house who has spent a life working other people's doors.

blocks.s: You ask him the thing you have wanted to ask since the porch. If the latch was never locked, if the door works both ways, why has he stood at it for two hundred years instead of lifting his own thumb and walking into the rain.
The latch clicks. He looks at the wedge of wet dark for a long moment.

"Because someone has to hold the door for the ones who can still leave," he says. "If I go, it shuts, and then the next one who comes down cold and will not be soothed finds only a wall, and stands in front of it not knowing it was ever a door, and goes back up to the warm because the wall told them to. I stay so the door stays a door. It is not nothing, holding a door. I told you at the top it was more of a job than it sounds." The latch clicks. "Now. You have seen it works. That changes the next part for you. Refuse the thing in your coat knowing the door is real, and you refuse it with your eyes open, which is the only refusal worth anything. Mind the step."

choices.label: Ask if he will hold the last door too, the one at the centre
Go back and face the Understudy, the door open behind you
Walk out now; take the door while it is open

blocks.s: You go to the empty shelf and you do the stupid thing, the human thing, which is to lay your hand flat on the stone where your back would go. It is cold and dry and very smooth, worn smooth not by a body but by being made ready, dusted, waited-over, kept aired for a visitor who is always just about to arrive. There is even, you see, a shallow dip worn into the stone where a head would rest, though no head has rested there yet. They wore the pillow-place into it in advance. They were that sure.

You take your hand back. The cold of the stone stays in your palm a while, and you find you are glad of it, because it is the realest thing you have touched since the rain, and a man should know what the warm is offering to spare him from before he decides whether he wants to be spared.

choices.label: Go to the figure in your coat

blocks.s: "And the second one," you say. It almost looks sorry to explain this one, which is how you know it is the worse of the two.

"You give me the face. All of it. I take your name and your bad knee and your creased photograph and your whole little remaining life, and I go up and out and live it, badly, the way you would have, and nobody out there ever knows the difference, least of all you. Because you stay. Down here, warm, kept, choosing nothing, ever again. Not the chair, not the vigil, not even your own ending. You become a guest. You sit in the gold upstairs with the others and you smile your too-still smile and you never, ever, have to decide anything, because deciding is the thing that hurts, and I would have taken it off you along with the face."

It watches you. "No grief. No goodbye. No arithmetic. You stop being the keeper and you stop being the chooser and you stop, in every way that matters, being a person with a thing to lose. It is the most comfortable thing I can offer you, and it is the worst, and I am required to tell you both."

choices.label: Ask what happens to the one you came for, if you wear no face
Ask what it would feel like, the not-choosing, the staying warm
Go find the Usher before you do anything you cannot undo
Take it. Give it your face. Stop having to choose

blocks.s: You take the first door. You let it sit your seat.

The relief is real and it is enormous and it is the most honest feeling you have had all night, which is the worst of it. The weight comes off your shoulders so fast you stagger, and the Understudy steadies you with your own warm hand, and smiles your half-beat-late smile, and rights the wrong collar one last time, and turns and goes down the cold stair toward the centre and the chair and the one you came for, to sit the vigil, to be the keeper, forever, so that you will not have to be.

You climb back up. You find the cold iron latch at the far end of the vault and it lifts under your thumb, just a latch, never locked, and the wet November night takes you and it is only ordinary cold, and the gate is where you left it, and the drive ends, and you walk out into the rest of your mortal life, free, aging, alone, having lost no one, having buried no one, having said no goodbyes.

It should feel like winning. Some nights, for years, it almost does. And then on the bad nights you remember that a person, more or less a person, made out of the part of you that wanted to rest, is sitting in the cold under that warm house wearing your coat, keeping the one you love from a death they may by now be begging for, forever, so that you would not have to be the one to grant it. You went free. You only sent someone in your place. You will never be sure that is different from what you came to undo.

You come back to where the Understudy sits between the sleeping elders, your coat, your face, the collar still wrong. It has not moved. It looks up at you with the patience of a thing that is only a want, and a want does not get bored.

"So," it says. "You have walked the cold end and looked at the lamp and done your arithmetic. You know the two doors I hold. Take-my-seat and you go free while I sit your forever. Wear-my-face and you stop being a person at all. Or you turn me down, and you keep the weight, and you go down that last cold stair to the cellar and the centre and you carry the whole unbearable thing yourself, to the one place where you finally have to set it down for real, on purpose, with your own two hands."

It stands. It rights the wrong collar and leaves it wrong on purpose, your gesture, learned and given back to you. "I will not fight you. I am the part of you that wants to rest. You are the part that keeps walking. Tell me which of us wins tonight."

choices.label: Take its seat; let it keep them; walk out free
Wear its face; stop having to choose, ever
Use the child against it before you decide
Listen to the slow clap that has started in the dark
Refuse the trade; go down to carry it yourself
Refuse it knowing full well the door was real

113.title: You Are the Part That Wanted To Set It Down

blocks.s: You tell it. The half-beat smile, the collar, the flinch it has not earned. You tell it you will always know, in any room, which of you is wearing the face wrong, because you are the one who has to live behind it.
It does not get angry. It looks, if anything, relieved, the way you are relieved when someone finally sees the thing you have been carrying alone.

"Good," it says. "Then you understand me better than anyone ever has. You are the part of me that kept walking. I am the part of you that wanted to set it down. We are the same coat, turned two different ways." It straightens the wrong collar, deliberately, leaving it wrong. "Which means when I make you the offer for real, you will know exactly how true it is, and you will refuse it anyway, and I will respect you for it and grieve you for it in the same breath. Go on. Choose. I cannot make you. That was never the deal."

choices.label: Offer it the one thing it actually wants: rest, a shelf, an end to wanting
Go find the Usher; learn whether the door out is real
Go straight to the choosing

114.title: The Last Door He Stands At

blocks.s: You ask him the thing that has been forming since he showed you the latch. There is a door at the centre, the one you are going down toward, the one the one you came for sits behind. Will he stand at that one too. Will he hold it open for you the way he holds this one.
The latch clicks. For the first time the thin man looks not at you but down at his own folded hands.

"I stand at every door in this house," he says. "That is the post. And I will tell you a true thing, because you came down cold and I am tired of standing at doors for people who did not. At the centre I have barred the way as often as I have opened it. Keepers come down with their grief in their hands and some of them, at the last door, I have judged not ready, and I have set my back against it for their own good, and turned them gently round, and sent them up to the warm to try again in a hundred years. I told myself it was mercy. I have begun to think it was only another lock, a kinder one, mine." The latch clicks. "So I cannot promise you I will open it. I can promise you I will be standing there. What I do when you arrive will depend on what kind of man comes down that stair, and that is not decided yet, and some of it is up to you. Mind the step."

choices.label: Go back and face the Understudy, knowing the last door is uncertain
Take this door now while it is plainly open; do not gamble on the last

115.title: The Child It Tries To Use

blocks.s: The Understudy sees you hesitating between the doors and plays its last card, the one it was always going to play, because it is your want and your want is not above this.

"There is a child," it says, gently, your gentlest voice. "You have seen her. Upstairs, frozen, the balloon, the blue light. Take my seat and the child stays warm. Wear my face and the child stays warm. Refuse me, go down, do the hard thing at the centre, and ask yourself what your hard thing does to a child who was promised forever and then watched the keeper choose an ending. You want to be the one who lets people finally rest. Fine. Start with her. See how it sits."

From somewhere far above, faint down the cold vault, comes the small dull tap of a balloon nosing a closed door. It is a cheap shot. It is your own cheap shot, turned on you, and it lands, because the cheap ones always land hardest when they are true.

choices.label: Make it look at the child honestly, the way you have to
Refuse it. The child deserves an honest end too, not endless warm
Refuse it, knowing the door is real and choosing the hard way anyway
It is too much. Take its seat. Let the child stay warm. Walk out
Let the seam decide; you saw it lie before and it is lying now

blocks.s: It steps fully into the lamplight, and it is wearing your face now, not just your coat. More or less. The way a passport photograph is more or less your face, flattened, a fraction off, the smile arriving a half-beat after the eyes. It holds itself the careful way you hold yourself when you are tired and pretending not to be, which is most of the time, which it has clearly studied.
It does not lunge. It does not threaten. It sits down on the edge of a stone shelf, companionably, between two sleeping elders, and pats the cold stone beside it.

"Finally," it says, in a voice that is nearly yours and getting nearer. "I have wanted to talk to you properly since the porch. Sit. It is the one room in this house where no one is listening, because no one down here can be made to want anything any more, and a thing that wants nothing makes a poor spy. Sit with me. I have an offer, and you are going to want to hear it, and then you are going to want to refuse it, and I would like you to do both honestly."

choices.label: Sit, and hear it out
Stay standing, but let it talk

gated.aside: It wears your thing worn a little wrong, and it looks at you and almost smiles, seeing itself. You are the part that wanted to stop choosing. So is it. You were always going to meet across a borrowed coat.
You can see the seam where its borrowed self meets the true one, because you have learned to find the joins, which means you will know the exact moment its kindness turns into the offer.

blocks.s: You speak into the dark where the clapping is, and you tell it you are not auditioning, you are here for one person and then you are leaving one way or another. The clap stops. The pencil does not.

When the voice comes it is very old and very dry and it does not raise itself, because it has never in two hundred years needed to be louder than the room. "They all say that," it says. "The ones worth keeping always say that. The ones who say take me, keep me, I will do anything, those I send to the shelves to wear smooth, because a thing that begs to be kept is already nothing. It is the ones who come down cold and say they are leaving that I want. You cannot be kept against the want; that is the one law even I obey. But you can be made to want it, given time, and I have nothing but time, and a swept shelf at your height, and the patience to clap for you across a hundred years until you walk to it yourself." The pencil scratches, a single decisive stroke. "Verdict: worth the wait. Go down, keeper. Refuse your little copy, carry your grief to the centre, do the brave hard thing. I will be clapping the whole way, and I can afford to lose tonight, because I do not lose centuries."

choices.label: Go down past the clapping; refuse the trade and carry it yourself
Go down knowing the door was real, the clap at your back
Let the verdict in; if the eldest wants you, perhaps you belong here

blocks.s: You ask the question it was hoping you would not. If you give away your face and become a guest who chooses nothing, who keeps the one you came for. Who sits the vigil.

It is quiet a moment. "No one," it says. "That is the part. In the wear-my-face trade nobody keeps them, because the keeper is the part of you that came down here on purpose, and you will have set that part down for good. They stay kept by the house, by inertia, by the table that is never cleared, slipping a little further from themselves every year the way the Prompter did, the way these elders did, with no one downstairs who remembers why they were worth coming for. You will be upstairs, warm, not remembering either. It is the cleanest trade for you and the cruelest for them. I told you it was the worse one. I do not enjoy being right about it."

choices.label: Go find the Usher; you have heard enough to want the door
Take it anyway; you are too tired to be a person any more

119.title: The One Who Decides Who Is Kept

blocks.s: There has been a sound under the conversation for a while now and you have been refusing to hear it, the same refusal you turn on the lilies' sweet underside upstairs. A clap. One pair of hands, slow, spaced, unhurried, somewhere in the deepest dark past the last lit niche, circling, never quite arriving in the lamplight, never meant to.

It is the eldest of the eldest, the one that does not lie down on a shelf, the one that decides. It is not clapping for the Understudy. It is clapping for you, appraising, the way a buyer claps a slow approving hand at a horse made to walk a circle. Between the claps comes the small dry scratch of a pencil, a verdict being drafted before the act is even finished.

"Go on," the Understudy murmurs, not looking around, your own voice gone careful. "It only does that for the ones it has decided are worth keeping. Most guests it never claps for at all. It thinks you are good casting. That is the most dangerous compliment in this house, and you have just earned it."

choices.label: Answer the dark; ask the eldest what its verdict is
Refuse to perform; turn your back on the clap and choose

120.title: The Same Coat, Set Down (an ending)

blocks.s: You do not take its seat and you do not give it your face. You give it the only thing it has never once been offered, because no keeper before you ever saw it clearly enough to offer it. You tell it to lie down.

You tell it that it is the part of you that is tired, and that you are not going to make it carry your chair forever any more than you would let it carry you out into the rain. You tell it that a want, granted nothing, can still be allowed to stop wanting. There is a swept shelf at the near end, at a height for a man of your build. You walk it there, your hand on its shoulder, your own warm shoulder under your own hand, and you help it lie down in your coat with the collar still turned wrong, and you smooth it straight at last, the one small mercy, and the borrowed face goes loose and grateful and very young.

"You walked all the way down," it says, your voice gone soft as a child's at the end of a long day, "to put the right part of us to bed. Nobody ever. Nobody ever did that." Its slow breath joins the slow breath of the eldest, one breath a minute, patient, at rest. You did not save the one you came for; you did not even reach them. But you reached this, the worst-tired piece of yourself, and you laid it down kindly, and you go on toward the centre lighter by exactly the weight of the part that wanted to quit, which is the heaviest weight a man carries and the last he ever sets down.

You ask what it costs the Understudy to sit the keeper's chair forever. It looks down the cold vault at the eldest on their shelves, the ones who have been kept longest, worn smoothest, the faces gone almost featureless with years.

"That," it says. "Eventually. The chair keeps the keeper too. You stop having a face of your own because you are too busy holding someone else's at the edge of a death you will not let finish. I would become that, slowly, in your coat, and the last thing to go would be the memory that I was ever the part of you that wanted to rest." It says it flatly, your flat tired voice. "I am offering to do that. Not because the house makes me. Because the alternative is watching you do it, and I would rather be the one in the chair than the one watching from the rain. That is the only love I am capable of, being the want and nothing else. Take it or do not. But do not tell me it is free. I am sitting right here telling you it is not."

choices.label: Go find the Usher; you will not decide blind
Take it anyway; let it pay; walk out

blocks.s: "Tell me the first one again," you say, and it leans in, glad to.

"You walk out. Tonight. Old age, taxes, a bad knee, all of it, the whole shabby mortal package you have been pretending you do not want back. And the one you love stays exactly as they are, warm, kept, not gone, never gone, with me in the keeper's chair beside them holding the vigil you cannot face holding forever. You get to grieve nobody. You get to bury nobody. You simply leave, and they simply stay, and I simply sit."

It watches you do the arithmetic. You hate that it can watch you do the arithmetic. The terrible part is that it is not a trick. You really could walk out. They really would stay. Somebody really would sit the chair. The only cost is that the somebody is a person, more or less, who would do it forever so that you would not have to do it at all.

choices.label: Ask what it costs IT to sit that chair forever
Ask it how it knows you would even take such a thing
Go look for the door before you decide it is even worth taking
Take it. Let it sit your seat. Walk out free

blocks.s: Names are cut into the stone over each niche, the lettering older the deeper you go, until the oldest are not letters you know at all. They are not death-dates. There are no death-dates anywhere down here, no second number after the first, because the second number is the one thing this house has spent everything to prevent. Only the year each one came in. Some of those years have four digits you would expect and some have three.

You understand, standing in the cold reading the kept-since dates of people who never got a died date, that you are reading the longest guest list in the world, and that there is a blank shelf at the near end, swept clean, waiting, at a comfortable height for a man of your build to lie down on. Nobody put your name over it. They did not have to. A swept shelf says it plainly enough.

choices.label: Look at the blank shelf they have left at your height
Turn your back on the shelf; go to the figure in your coat

blocks.s: You ask the obvious thing, the thing a tired man asks a salesman. What does it get. It goes still, your stillness, and for a moment the borrowed face is not performing anything.

"I get to set something down too," it says, quieter. "You think I am the house's hand. I am not. I am the part of you that wanted to put this whole thing down on the porch and walk into the warm and never come out. The house made a person of that part, because it is a useful part to have wandering the halls in your coat, smiling at you. I am the want, given a body. When I offer to take your seat, I am not lying to you. I would genuinely rather sit it than watch you carry it. That is the cruelty of me. I am the kindest lie you tell yourself, and I mean every word."

choices.label: Press it on take-my-seat
Press it on wear-my-face
Go and find the Usher; you need to know if the door even opens

blocks.s: You ask it the dangerous question, the one you should not ask a salesman, which is what it would actually feel like. To stay warm. To not choose. To set the whole thing down for good.

It closes your eyes for a moment, the slow private way of someone remembering a good day. "Like the first warm minute under the portico," it says, "the one where the cold of the drive ended and you had not yet remembered why you came. You felt it. I was there; I am the part of you that wanted to stay in that minute. It would feel like that, forever. No nine-past-nine on a dead man's wrist ticking at you. No arithmetic behind your teeth. No bad night where you do the sums on who you can still save and come up short. You would sit by the fire and someone would always have just refilled your glass and you would never once have to be brave again."

It opens your eyes. "And here is the part I am required to say, because I am the kind of lie that says it. The reason you would never have to be brave again is that there would no longer be a you to do the being. The warm does not carry you over the hard part. It dissolves the part of you that finds it hard. You would not endure peace; you would become a thing that cannot be disturbed, which is not the same as a man at rest. I would know. I am the want, and the want, granted, stops being anybody at all."

choices.label: Ask what happens to the one you came for, then
Go find the Usher; you have heard enough warmth to want the cold
Take it anyway; let yourself become a thing that cannot be disturbed

blocks.s: There is another voice in the cold, flat, unhurried, coming from no particular niche, an old thrall worn down to almost nothing, the one that has been finishing your sentences since the long table. It speaks before you do, your exact words, a half-beat early.

"Both doors lead to a seat," it says, in your voice, saying the thing you were about to think. "That is the part it does not put in the offer. Take-my-seat and your seat is filled and the chair has its body. Wear-my-face and you are the body in the chair upstairs. There is no door it holds that empties a chair. There is only the chair that does the refusing, and that one it cannot offer you, because that one costs." The flat voice pauses, and then, rare, says something that is not yours, something it found for itself in two hundred years of cold: "It knows. The understudy knows it leads to a seat. So does the one who built it the offer to give you. Ask yourself who taught a copy of a man to sell him his own chair, and whether that one ever once sat anything else."
Then it is quiet, spent, the way it goes quiet after a true cue, and will not say more.

choices.label: Press it on take-my-seat now you know where it leads
Press it on wear-my-face now you know where it leads
Go find the Usher; you want a door that is not a chair

127.title: The Far End of the Vault

blocks.s: You leave the Understudy on its stone shelf, and it lets you, because it is the want and the want is patient, and you go down the long cold vault past the sleeping eldest toward the far end, where the lamp does not reach.

There is a draught here. A real one, thin and cold, carrying the smell of wet November gravel, which means somewhere past these stones there is an outside, an actual outside, the rain you came in out of. And standing in the draught, away from his warm door, in the one cold place in the whole house, is the thin man, the doorman, his hands folded, waiting as if he had heard you coming, which he had.

A small dry click, a thumb on a catch in his sleeve, comes a half-second before he speaks. "You found the cold end," he says. "Good. The warm end is no use to you now. Mind the step; the floor drops."

choices.label: Ask him what the draught is, where the cold air comes from
Ask him, but you feel the warmth in your gut pull you back

blocks.s: Before the thing in your coat can start, you do the one practical thing the long descent has taught you, which is to check your own pockets. You have come a long way down through a house that takes pieces of people, and you would like to know what is still yours.

The creased photograph is there against your chest, the daylight world folded small. The dead woman's beads are there, the little cross worn smooth, the faint cigarette smell of a man two hundred years gone. And the warmth in your own stomach, the iron warmth or the absence of it, tells you plainly whether you drank at the table or kept your edge. Whatever you brought, you still have. The crypt did not take it. The crypt, it seems, takes nothing; it only keeps.

choices.label: Walk the vaults and look at the eldest
Step toward the figure in your coat

blocks.s: You ask about the draught, but even as you ask it the iron warmth in your stomach, the cup you took at the table, turns you gently by the shoulders back toward the lamp and the company and the comfortable dark. The cold air does not feel like escape to you. It feels like a draught under a door that someone really ought to see to, an unpleasantness, a thing to be away from.

The thin man watches you not-quite-feel it. The latch clicks, soft and sorry. "Ah," he says. "You drank. I thought you might have. The far door is still there. I am still standing at it. But you cannot want it, not the way you would have to want it to walk through it, because the warmth in you wants the warmth in the house, and that is the whole of how the keeping works. I am sorry. I cannot show a man a door he is no longer able to see."
He steps aside anyway, out of courtesy, onto nothing you can make yourself look at. "Go back. Choose what you can still choose. It is more than nothing. Mind the step."

choices.label: Go back to the Understudy and choose what you can
Sink down here in the cold and let the warmth decide for you

blocks.s: The stair from the crypt lets you out into the last room before the centre, and it is not what the word cellar led you to expect. It is clean. It is warm, the steady warmth of a place kept at one temperature for a long time, and along the walls stand the racks of a wine cellar, and what is racked is not wine. It is decanted into glass, ruby and still, each bottle labelled in a small old hand, dated, named, a cellar of kept blood laid down like vintage against the years of a feast that does not end.

In the middle of it, at a plain table under one lamp, sits a figure with a pencil in its hand and a ledger open in front of it, and it is the oldest thing you have seen in this house, older than the table, older than the cold in the crypt. It is going down the column of names with the pencil, slow, ticking.
It does not look up. It finishes the line it is on. Then it sets the pencil down, square to the edge of the ledger, and only then does it look at you.

choices.label: Wait for it to speak first
Say you have come for the one downstairs
Look at the labelled bottles on the nearest rack

blocks.s: "What I did." The pencil turns once in its fingers. "I am the one who turns them. The eldest does the turning; it is too much to ask of the young, and the young flinch, and a flinch at that moment is a cruelty. So they bring them to me, the dying, the loved, the ones nobody can bear to let go, and I do the work, and it does not hurt, and afterward they do not die. That is the gift. I am very good at it. I have been very good at it for a long time."

It looks at you with the worn coin of its face. "I turned the one you came for. On the night you asked. You stood where you are standing now, near enough, with them dying in the ordinary way in your arms, and you asked me to make it stop, and I told you the price, the whole of it, and you did not hear it, because no one hears it, and I did the work anyway, because you asked. I want that said. Say who asked."

choices.label: Say it: you asked. Take your part of it
Refuse it: it lied, it took them, the asking does not count
Try to bargain: undo it, and you will owe whatever it wants

blocks.s: From among the racks, from the dark behind the labelled glass, the flat voice comes, the one that finished your thoughts a half-beat early all the way up in the music room. It is fainter now, and it has to reach for the words it used to find before you did, and the reaching costs it; you can hear the cost in the flatness.

"...the one who turns," it says, finishing the thought you were having about the old figure at the table. "It turned them. Yes. But ask yourself, while you have the wit left to ask anything down here, who turned the one who turns." A long flat pause; this is not a finished thought of yours, this is one of its own rare originals, and it spends itself to say it. "No one. That is the answer and it is the wrong answer. There was a first. There is always a first. The first one stood at a grave and could not walk away and did not get turned, because there was no one older to do it; it had to do something else with the not-walking-away, something larger, and it is still doing it, upstairs, all warm and courteous, and it has never once stood at its own."

choices.label: Ask the voice to say plainly who it means

blocks.s: The other one is the girl from the landing, the newest-turned, the one who told you not to get comfortable. She is here in the cellar now, sat on the floor against the cool racks with her knees up the way you first found her, and the dry is all gone out of her. She is not flinching any more. That is the worst thing you could have been shown about her, worse than fear.

"They got me to stop," she says, when she sees you, flat. "You were up there. You heard me say I didn't want to stop. Give it long enough, they said. Well." She looks at her own still hands. "It isn't even sad now. That's the trick of it. It's just long. I wanted to use up. I wanted to be tired and have it count. Instead I'm down here being twenty-two forever in a room full of bottles with people's names on them, and one of these days one of them will have my name on it, and I'll help label it, and I won't mind. That's what kept past wanting is. You stop wanting. They wait you out."
She hums the three notes, once, and they do not shake at the top any more, and somehow that is the most frightening sound in the house.

choices.label: Ask if there is anything she still wants
You have seen enough; you cannot bear it; turn back
Go on toward the centre door with what you have seen

blocks.s: You cannot. She is not the one you came for. You came down all this way for one person and you have so little left in you that you ration it, and you tell yourself, in the warm clean cellar, that you cannot spend any of it on a girl you met on a landing, that you have to save it for the chair at the centre, that this is not cruelty but care.

You hold back. You let the moment close. The dry comes up in her one last time, thin and brave and then gone, and she nods, slow, as if you have only confirmed a thing she already knew about everyone who comes through. "Yeah," she says, flat. "That's the trick of it. Everybody's saving it for the one they came for. That's how they keep us all. Off you go."

She hums the three notes and they do not shake and they do not fall, and you carry the sound down the last stair with you, and it does not leave, not at the centre, not after, not ever, the small even hopeless sound of the one mercy you could have done and would not, because you were saving yourself for a larger one you are no longer sure you have it in you to do. You went on. You should not have gone on like this. But you did.

Past the racks, past the lamp and the closed ledger, there is one more door, lower than the rest, plain wood worn pale at the height of a hand, and beyond it, you know without being told, is the centre, and the chair, and the one you came for. The Usher is there already, ahead of you, standing at the low door as he stood at the high one a whole house ago, hands folded, waiting.

The latch clicks under his thumb a half-beat before he speaks. "You have seen it, then," he says, and it is not a question. "The child. The girl. What keeping is. Good. I would not let you through if you had not. I have held this door for a long line of keepers and the ones I let through unseeing are the ones I grieve." He inclines his head. "They are just beyond. Warm. Exactly as the night you said yes. Whatever you do in there, you will do it knowing what the alternatives cost. That is all I can give you. Mind the step."

choices.label: Take the last steps down to the centre door
Stand at the door; you are not sure you can do this

blocks.s: You keep refusing. You will not be made to confess to a thing that was done to you in your worst hour; you keep your hands clean and your argument sharp and you win it, point by point, in the warm clean room, against the oldest thing in the house.

And the oldest thing in the house begins, slowly, to clap. A slow deliberate clap, palm to palm, the verdict it gives to what it finds worth keeping forever. "Yes," it says, with the appetite no longer hidden at all. "Oh, yes. Not soft. Not sorry. An edge that will not be worn down by a cellar full of the truth. Do you know how rarely one of you comes down here and wins? I keep those. I keep them right here, where I can reach them, my best vintage."

It rises, and it is faster than a thing that old should be, and it is between you and the low door before you have finished the next clever thing you were going to say. There is a chair drawn up for you at the table by the lamp, beside the ledger, and a glass laid down years ago against exactly this night, ruby and warm. You argued so well. You will have a long, long time to keep arguing. The one at the centre waits, uncome-for, while the eldest pulls out your chair with the courtesy it shows the things it values most, which are the things it never, ever lets go.

You crouch down to the small chair, the way you crouched at the small frame upstairs a lifetime of stairs ago, and you make yourself look at the child, and the child looks back at you with the patient curiosity of the very young.
"Is it real?" it asks. The same question. Not afraid. It taps the soft balloon against the arm of the chair, once, a dull small sound, and waits, as if you of anyone might finally know.

You do not lie to it. You have done enough soft kind lying tonight by standing in a house built out of one. "No," you say. "None of this is real. You were supposed to get to be real. Somebody loved you so much they kept you instead." The child considers this with great seriousness, and taps the balloon again, and does not cry, because it has forgotten how, and that is the part you will carry up the last stair with you, that it did not even know to cry.

choices.label: Stand, and go to the other one it kept

blocks.s: It takes you through a low arch into a side of the cellar you had not seen, and it shows you, and it does not soften it, and the not-softening is a mercy of a kind, because there is no way to dress this up that would not be worse.

There is a small chair. The child from the upstairs window is in it, the one with the balloon, frozen at the age it was the night it was kept, and it is not asleep and it is not sad. It is nothing. It looks up at you and it is still, the way the still ones upstairs are still, except that a child should never be that still, and the foil balloon on its wrist has gone soft and low and has not been changed because there is no point changing it, because the birthday it was for will not come again and will never stop being almost here.

"This one," the figure says, "a mother asked for, the same as you asked. It will be a child as long as there is a house. It cannot grow and it cannot grieve and it cannot be grieved, because nothing here is allowed to die. That is what keeping is. Not saving. Keeping. You may look at it as long as you can stand to, and then I will show you the other one."

choices.label: Make yourself look, and ask the child if it is real
Turn from the child to the other one it means
Listen to the house above you while you stand here

gated.aside: You make yourself look, and keep looking, the way nobody else in this house can bear to. That steadiness is the thing the coven cannot give and cannot fake. It is also the thing that will let you do what comes next.
The frozen child will not age and will not be grieved, and you know that arithmetic from the inside of it. Kept is not the same as saved. You have been on the wrong side of that sum.

blocks.s: It is neither man nor woman now, only old, the face worn like a coin too long in circulation, the features mostly gone and the value still legible. It folds its hands on the closed ledger.
The pencil-scratch comes first, one short stroke on the cover of the book, a small sound to clear the silence, and then the verdict, even and unhurried.

"You are the keeper of the one in the centre," it says. "I know you. I have your name in the book. I have had it a long time." It tips its head. "You think you came down here to reach them. You came down here to reach me. I am the one you have to get past, and the only toll I take is that you hear, from the one who did it, exactly what was done, and that you say, out loud, who asked for it."

choices.label: Ask it plainly what it did

140.title: Somebody Should Sit With It (an ending)

blocks.s: You sit down on the cellar floor beside the small chair, because the girl on the landing said it once and she was right: somebody should sit outside a child's door, somebody should sit beside a child that nothing can be done for, and there is no one else in this whole warm house who will, because to sit here you have to admit there is something wrong with a child that does not grow.

You admit it. You sit. The child taps the soft balloon against the arm of the chair, once, and looks at you, and you stay, and the staying feels, for a little while, like the only honest thing you have done since the rain.

But the one you came for is not the child. They are still in the centre, in the warm, kept, uncome-for, and every hour you sit here being kind to the frozen child is an hour you are not facing the door you came all this way to face. The old figure does not disturb you. It has seen this before, the keeper who gets within one room of the centre and finds a smaller grief to tend so as not to tend the large one. It lets you stay. The house is glad to have you. There is, after all, no hurry, and now there never will be.

You go through the low door, and you go down the last short flight, and the cellar and its racks and its closed ledger and the old figure standing in the wake of a death it has no name for close over behind you.

You know now what keeping did. You have seen the child that will not grow and the girl who was waited out, or who was finally let to fall, and you carry the seeing with you, COVEN-WITNESSED, the weight you could not face the centre without. You know now, too, the thing the house kept from you the whole way down and could not keep from you here: that to free the one you love is to let them die, a real death, the kind that is allowed in the daylight world and nowhere in this one, and that there is no undoing it and only an allowing.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm, close, from the wood of the door itself:

Almost there. They are just beyond, and they are exactly as you left them, which is the whole of what I have ever offered anyone. Come and see. There is no hurry. There has never been any hurry. Come and sit with them, and we will talk about forever.

blocks.s: The last door is in front of you. Beyond it is the chair, and the one you kept, warm, waiting, and the choice you came all this way down to make.
Before you turn to the table you take stock of yourself, an old habit from the daylight world that the warm house has not quite worn out of you, the kind of thing you do at the top of a hard task.

Against your chest, buttoned in the inside pocket, the photograph is still there, the creased one, the Tuesday with the real sun in it, the square of the world that ages and ends. You did not lose it on the long way down. And in your fist, or your other pocket, the worn beads with the small smooth cross are still there too, the rosary out of a dead smoker's coat, the thing that would not be soothed. You came down lighter than you came in, but not empty. You kept the two cold true things, and you are glad of them now, here at the bottom, in the warm clean cellar.

choices.label: Turn to the figure at the table

blocks.s: You ask the flat voice to say it plainly, the thing it is circling, and there is the longest pause it has ever given you, the pause of something going through its own emptied pockets for a coin it knows is not there.

"...plainly," it says, and you can hear it spending the last of whatever lets it speak at all. "I cannot. Not because I will not. Because I gave you the true cue, the real one, the one I was keeping, and a thing like me only has the one, and now it is spent, and I will go back to finishing your sentences and never again my own." Fainter. "You have it, though. You heard it. The first keeper. The one who could not walk away from a grave. Put it down beside what the eldest would not tell you, and carry both up unanswered, and do not, whatever you do, ask the Curator a kind question at the centre. It will answer. The answer will be a courtesy. The courtesy will cost you the only thing you have left to spend."
The voice goes quiet, all the way quiet, the way a thing goes quiet that has just used itself up for you.

choices.label: Carry it unanswered to the centre door

blocks.s: You have nothing of the world in your pockets; you came down too light, or too soothed, to carry it. So you turn to the old figure by the lamp and you ask it, the one who does the turning, to do the other thing too: to let her stop.

It is quiet a long moment. "I do not do that," it says. "I have never once done that. I turn. I keep. The other way is not mine." The pencil-scratch comes, slow, on the cover of the ledger. "But you are asking me to, with her in front of me, asking too. Two of you asking. That is a thing that has not happened down here before." It looks at the girl, and at you, and then it does a thing you do not understand the cost of yet: it reaches out and closes its own worn hand over the girl's, gently, a parent settling a child to sleep, and it lets her go.

The three notes rise and, for the first time, fall. The girl is still. The old figure draws its hand back and looks at it as if the hand belonged to someone else, and for a moment, just one, the worn face is not old and not even, but stricken, the face of something that has remembered what it spent centuries not looking at.

choices.label: Watch the old figure, who has just done the thing it never does

blocks.s: You rise. The girl is gone, properly gone, the first real absence in a house that does not allow them, and the cellar is very quiet around the place where she stopped being.

The old figure has not moved from the lamp. It is looking at the empty floor where she sat, and its worn face is doing something you have not seen it do, a thing that on a younger face you would have to call grief, except that grief admits a death, and nothing in this house is allowed to admit one, and so on its face it looks like a wound it has no name for.

"You did that with love," it says at last, not to you exactly. "I had begun to believe it could not be done with love. Only with cruelty, or with carelessness, and I have a great deal of both, and neither of them was ever any use to me at the centre." It picks up the pencil and does not write anything. "Go on, then. You will need to know it can be done with love, where you are going. You will need it more than you can know."

choices.label: Ask why it lingers on the empty floor
Go toward the centre door
Sit down where she sat; you have nothing left for the centre

146.title: All of It on Her (an ending)

blocks.s: You sit down where she sat, against the cool racks, in the place her absence is, and you find that you cannot get up again, because you have just done the unthinkable with love and it took everything, and there is nothing left over for the larger one waiting in the centre.

It was real. It was the truest thing you have done in this house and maybe in your life, and it has emptied you out completely, and the centre is one room away and might as well be a hundred floors. You will rest first. You only need to rest. You did such a hard kind thing; surely you are allowed to rest.

The old figure looks at you with something close to pity, because it knows this one too: the keeper who spends the whole of themselves on the mercy they could do and has none left for the mercy they came to do. The house draws warm around you. You did one true thing. You will tell yourself, sitting here, that one true thing is enough, and the one you came for will wait in their chair while you decide, forever, that you have already done your part.

You read the labels on the nearest rack while the old figure watches you do it. The hand is careful, the dates impossible. One bottle is dated in a year you would have to count back centuries to reach. Another is recent, the ink barely set, and the name on it is a girl's name, and you do not let yourself wonder yet whose.

On a low shelf, apart from the rest, set where it will be reached for and not lost in the racks, stands a smaller flask. Beside it, incongruous, is a thing from the daylight world: a child's cup, the cut-crystal kind, with a little juice gone dark and thick in the bottom of it, set down years ago by someone who meant to bring it back up to be washed and never did. The juice has gone to syrup. The cup has not been touched.

Pinned to the end of the rack, yellow and curling, is a paper inventory in a clerk's neat hand, columns of dates and quantities, and three quarters of the way down someone has totted a sum in the margin, got it wrong by a clear forty, and never gone back to fix it. Forty of something, off, for centuries, because the one who kept the books got bored or got turned or simply could not be bothered, and the error sits there being an error, patient, mundane, nobody's, the most ordinary thing in the cellar.
The figure at the table speaks without turning. "You may look. Everything down here is kept. That is the whole of what we do."

choices.label: Take stock of what you carried down here
Listen to the voice that comes from behind the racks
Turn to the figure at the table

blocks.s: The old figure stands at the edge of the lamplight and does not return to its ledger. You watch it, and you begin to understand that helping you, or letting the girl stop, or simply standing here in the wake of a real death, is costing it something, the way a held breath costs you, the only such image you will let yourself reach for in this cold clean room.

"I keep what I find worth keeping," it says. "I have kept everyone in this house. There is one I could never keep, because it was older than me and would not be turned, and it learned the keeping from watching me do it, and it does not turn people. It keeps something larger. It keeps the grief itself. It stages all of ours so very beautifully." The pencil-scratch. "I have said too much. I am allowed to say too much tonight, just this once, because you let her go and the letting-go has loosened something in me I had bricked up a long time ago. Do not tell the one upstairs I told you. It does not know that I know what it is. Or it knows, and lets me think it does not, which is the same courtesy, and costs the same."

choices.label: Ask it directly what the Curator is
Let it keep its silence; go on to the centre

gated.aside: One judge waits on another's verdict, and it would spend itself to grant yours, if you gave it something worth the cost. You know exactly what that costs. You are the same appetite, wearing a kinder face.

blocks.s: You ask her, low, whether there is anything left that she wants. She is a long time answering. The old figure stands back by the lamp and lets her, which tells you the old figure has heard this before and is not afraid of it.

"One thing," she says at last. "The thing I wanted on the landing, before they wore it out of me. I want to be let stop. Properly. The way you're not allowed." She looks up at you and for one moment the dry comes back, thin, brave. "I asked, you know. I'm the only one who keeps asking. They tell me it's a phase. Two hundred years of phase, they keep promising me. But I think you could do it, if you wanted. You've got the daylight on you. I can smell it. You brought a piece of the world that ends in here with you. I think it would work on me."
She does not beg. She has too much left of herself, still, to beg. She just says it, and waits, and the old figure watches you both and says nothing.

choices.label: Use the daylight you carried in, and let her stop
You have a ward; use the true cross of it, and let her stop
You have nothing of the world on you; ask the Critic to do it
You cannot. You came for one person, not her. Hold back

blocks.s: "I asked." You make yourself say it, in the warm clean room, to the worn old face. You asked. You stood with them going and you could not stand it and you asked for this, all of it, the racks and the labelled years and the feast that never clears, you asked for the part of it you understood and the much larger part you did not.
