The figure does not gloat. That is the worst of it. It nods, once, a clerk settling a long-overdue line in a book.

"There," it says. "That is the toll. Most cannot pay it. Most stand here and tell me they were tricked, and it is even true, in the way that a thing can be true and not the truth. You paid it. Now you may see what your asking bought. Come. I will not soften it. You have earned the unsoftened version."

choices.label: Go where it leads you, to see

blocks.s: You take out the photograph, the creased one, the Tuesday with the real sun in it that you have carried against your chest the whole way down, and you put it in her still hands and fold her still fingers over it, and you tell her to look at the sun in it, the one that ages things, the one that is allowed to end them.

She looks. The sun in the small square is only ink and old light, but it is the world's sun, and she came from the world once, and the part of her that did has not been waited out after all, only buried. Her breath, which she does not need, goes out of her, and she lets it, and she does not take the next one. The three notes start, rising, and this time at the top they do not shake and they do not hold; they fall. They are allowed to fall. The dim cellar takes the last of them.
It is the first death this house has been permitted in longer than you can count, and it was done with love, and it can be done, and now you know it can.

choices.label: Close her hands on the photograph and rise

blocks.s: You have no daylight on you, but you have the beads, the worn rosary out of a dead smoker's coat, the thing the woman carried who walked out of this house with her husband in a box and her whole grief still ahead of her. You take it out and the little cross presses into your palm the way it has the whole way down.

You do not pray; you would not know how, and it is not the prayer that is the ward. It is the refusal in it, the cold true edge that would not be soothed. You close her still hand around the cross and the smell of someone else's cigarettes, the real long-dead world, comes off it into the cellar.

She holds it. The fight she had been keeping up for a hundred years against being kept finds, at last, something to push off from. Her breath goes out and she lets it, and the three rising notes lift and, for the first time in this house, fall, and are done. The old figure by the lamp lowers its worn face. Even it, you think, has not seen this often.

choices.label: Take the beads back, and rise

153.title: There Must Be a Price (an ending)

blocks.s: You insist. There is always a price; everything in this house has one; you press the old figure to name what it will take to undo the turning, your years, your seat, your blood in a bottle on the rack with your name in the careful hand.

It watches you press, and the worn face settles into something almost like pity. "You still think this is a market," it says. "You will go to the centre thinking it, if I let you. You will stand in front of the one you love and you will try to pay for them, and the house will let you, because there is always something more to pay, and the paying never ends, and a keeper who is busy paying never has to do the one thing that cannot be bought." The pencil-scratch. "No. I will not send you to the centre like this. You are not ready, and you have shown me you will not get ready, and an unready keeper at the centre is the saddest thing I make."

It does not keep you the way it keeps the worthy. It does something gentler and worse: it lets you stay at the table, bargaining, drawing up terms, refining your offer, certain that the next one will be the one it accepts. You are still there now. You will always be there now, a step from the low door, sure that the deal is nearly done, while the one you came for waits in a chair you never reach, kept, and unpaid for, forever.
You ask it directly. What is the Curator. Who is the one who poured the welcome over you at the door, who calls itself by that name, who runs the house.
The old figure closes the ledger. The pencil-scratch comes, final this time, the stroke it makes before a verdict it intends to stand by.

"No," it says. "That is the one question I will not answer, and not because I am forbidden. Because if I say it, it becomes a thing you know, and a thing you know is a thing you can be made to use, and you are going to the centre, and at the centre you must be thinking about the one in the chair and nothing else. Not about the house. Not about who built it. Only about them, and what love is, and whether it can let a person end." It sets the pencil down, square to the edge. "Carry the question up the stair unanswered. It is lighter that way, I promise you, than carrying the answer."

choices.label: Accept that, and go on to the centre door

blocks.s: You try to bargain. Undo it, you tell it. Put them back the way they were, before the turning, and you will pay whatever the figure names, your years, your blood, your seat, anything.
The pencil goes still. For a moment you think you have found the one door in this house that opens.

"Undo it," it repeats, tasting the word. "You want me to make them mortal again. To give them back the dying I took. Child, that is the one thing in this house that costs nothing, and it is the one thing you will not be able to do when it is offered to you for free. I cannot undo it. No one can undo it. It can only be ended. There is a difference between undoing a death and allowing one, and you do not yet know it, and you will not learn it from me at a discount. Put your purse away. The toll is not money. The toll is the truth, and you have just tried to buy your way around it."

choices.label: Stop bargaining; say the true thing
Insist there must be a price; press it

blocks.s: You have seen it now, the child and the girl, what keeping is and is not, and that is precisely why you cannot make yourself go through the low door. You turn around. You go back up, past the racks and the labelled years, past the old figure that does not stop you, up the long way you came, toward the warm.

It is worse, going up, than it would have been never to have come down. Before, you could tell yourself you did not know. Now you know, and you are going back to the chair by the fire anyway, because knowing turned out not to be the same as being able to bear it. The warmth takes you in again at the head of the stair, gladder than ever, because the house loves nothing better than a keeper who has seen the whole cost and chosen the comfort anyway; that one it never has to worry about again.

They settle you in the deep chair. Someone tucks the fur across your knees. Far below, the one you came for waits in the centre, kept, uncome-for, and you sit in the warm with the seeing still in you, and the seeing is the only cold thing left in you, and the house has all the time it needs to wait even that out.
You stand at the low door and you tell the thin man the truth, which is that you do not know if you can do whatever it is that is waiting on the other side of it.

The latch clicks. "No one knows," he says. "Not one keeper in all the years I have held this door has known, going in. That is not a reason to stay on this side. There is only one thing worse than going through that door not knowing what you will do, and it is the thing the house wants of you, which is to stand here in the warm forever, not knowing, and call the not-deciding peace." He steps aside, the way he stepped aside on the porch. "You witnessed. You may go through. Or you may go back up to the chair by the fire and be comfortable. The latch works both ways; I showed you that two rooms ago, or someone did. Choose the door, not the chair."

choices.label: Go through the low door to the centre
Turn back up toward the warm and the chair

blocks.s: "It was you," you tell it. "You took them. You dress the taking up in a price I could not have understood and then you call the not-understanding consent. A dying person in my arms is not a contract. The asking does not count."
The pencil-scratch comes again, longer this time, appraising. The worn face does something that might, on a younger face, have been a smile.

"How well you argue," it says, and there is appetite in the evenness now, the first you have heard from it. "How clean you keep your hands. Do you know, that is exactly the quality I keep. Not the soft ones. The ones with edges. The ones who can stand in a blood-cellar and win the argument. I find those worth keeping forever." It opens the ledger again and finds your line. "You will not see what I keep until you stop performing for me. But I am in no hurry. I have a chair for you, and an excellent vintage laid down, and all the time there has ever been."

choices.label: Keep refusing; you will not be made to confess to it
Stop. Drop the argument. Say the true thing instead

blocks.s: You stand by the small chair and for a moment you let yourself hear the whole house stacked over your head, all the warm floors you came down through. Very far up, faint through the stone, there is a count, slow, patient, a big man holding his hunger off one number at a time at a cold grate; he is still up there, still deciding, still not coming down. Somewhere between you and him there is a footstep that walks the way you walk, in your own coat with the collar turned wrong, the understudy that wanted your seat, drifting the crypt now that you have passed it, neither defeated nor won, only waiting to see which of you ends up in the chair.

The sounds of the upper house are the sounds of choices not yet made. Down here, by the frozen child, there are no choices left to anyone; that is what the bottom of this house is. Everything above is still deciding. Everything down here is decided, and kept.

choices.label: Sit down by the child; you cannot leave it alone down here
Make yourself go on to the other one the Critic kept

blocks.s: The stair ends. There is no more down to go. A short stone passage, blood-warm again after the cold of the cellar, runs the last few yards to a plain wooden door, and from under it comes a thin line of candlelight and the smell of a room kept shut and loved too long, lilies and the sweet rot beneath them, and one note that is only theirs, that you would know in the dark of any house in the world.

You have come all the way down for this. You carry what you carried; the cold of what you saw further up is still on you, the frozen child, the girl kept past wanting, the things this house calls keeping. You carry the line the Usher drew at the start, that the truth is the bait here, and you have decided to walk into the bait anyway, because the one you love is on the other side of that door and you are not leaving them alone in it.
The thin man is already at the door. Of course he is. He was at the door the night you said yes; he is at this one now, hands folded, the latch under his thumb.

choices.label: Tell him what you saw on the way down
Take a moment first; let the souls you passed catch up to you
Go straight to the door

gated.aside: You come into the warm centre and your hand is already half lifted toward the empty seat, the way it once reached for a smaller hand that was not there. You have been walking toward this chair the whole way down.
Everything in your closed hand wants to stay. You came here built to hold on, and the house is only asking you to keep doing the thing you are best at. That is precisely the trap.

161.title: The One Who Wanted Your Place

blocks.s: You stay, but you do not take the chair, because there is someone who has wanted it more honestly than you ever did, and you call them in.

The one who has worn your coat all the way down comes through the door with the collar still turned wrong on the one side you would never turn, your face nearly your face on theirs, and they look at the empty warm chair across from your beloved the way you have been looking at it since the porch, with a hunger you finally recognise because it has been yours.

"You'd really let me," they say. Not your voice, quite. Close. "You'd give me the seat. The forever. Them." And for once they are not mimicking you; they are just wanting, openly, the thing you both want, and you nod, and they sit, careful, reverent, into your warmth, and your beloved turns to them with the old smile already on, because the chair is what the smile is for, not who is in it. The Understudy has your place and your eternity and your beloved's too-warm hand in theirs, and they are, at last, content, and the awful gentle truth is that your beloved cannot tell, and will never tell, and would not mind.

You step back into the cold of the passage, free, with neither the death you came to give nor the forever you came tempted by, having handed your whole story to someone who wanted it more, and the door, working both ways, closes on the warm without a sound.

You ask them the question you have not let yourself ask all the way down. Whether this is them. Whether there is anyone here at all, or only the shape the house keeps in their chair so you will keep coming back to it.

They are quiet a moment, turning the untouched glass. "I don't know," they say, and it is the most them thing they have said, because they always told you the truth even when a kindness would have been easier. "I remember us. I remember the Tuesday. I remember being afraid, before, and then not being afraid, and I've been not afraid for a long time now, and I can't tell any more if that's peace or if it's just what's left when they take the afraid out." They look at you. "I know I'm tired in a way I'm not allowed to be. I know there's a chair across from me that's been empty a long time and I'm glad it isn't now. Whether that's me or the house wanting you to stay, I genuinely cannot tell you. I don't think I get to know that any more. I think you might be the only one of us who still can."

choices.label: Ask the Curator to call it; you cannot do this alone
Decide it yourself, with them, here

blocks.s: You refuse. Not the chair, and not the letting-go; both. You will not take their hand to keep them and you will not take their hand to end them. You will not let the house make this a thing with two warm doors and a curtain. You stand, and you take your hand off the back of the empty chair, and you take it back from the table, and you say, to the room, to the candle-smoke, to the thing in it: no. Not on these terms. Not on any terms it set. You did not come down here to choose between the deaths it is offering you. You came down here for a person, and the person is not a choice on a menu, and you refuse to treat them like one.

The room does not know what to do with that. For the first time the warmth has no answer ready; the fire wavers; the food at the table seems, for a moment, to be exactly what it is, which is old and ruined and never to be eaten.

lines: THE CURATOR, and under the courtesy, for the first time, something that might be delight, or might be hunger, or might be both:

Oh. Oh, the one who refuses. There is always one who will not take the door I hold or the door I hide, who insists on a third thing I have not built. I confess I adore you. I have no idea what becomes of you; that is precisely the pleasure of it. You have walked off my map, keeper, and I have so very few maps left to walk off. Do let us see where you land.

blocks.s: What is on the far side of refusing the wager, you do not know. Neither does the house. You take the one you love by the hand, on no one's terms but your own, and you walk toward a door that may not be there, into a dark the Curator did not stage, with the cost of it entirely unknown and entirely yours.

You do not ask him for anything. You reach past his folded hands and put your own on the latch and open the door yourself, because you are done being held and warned and shown; you came down here to do a thing and you will at least start it with your own hand.

The thin man lets you. He steps back the exact width he needs to and no more, and the latch clicks under your fingers and not his for the first time tonight, and something passes over his old face that you have not seen on it, which might be relief.
"As you like," he says. "It is your door more than mine, in truth. It always was. I only ever held it." He inclines his head. "Mind the step."
The door swings in on candlelight, and the smell of them comes out to meet you, and you go in.

choices.label: Go in

blocks.s: It is a small room, and warm, and there is nothing terrible in it, which is the most terrible thing about it. A low fire. A round table set for two, with food on it that no one has eaten and no one will, going slowly to the syrup and the wax that food goes to when it is kept and not eaten. Two chairs. One of them is theirs.
They look up.

It is them. Not a copy, not a thing wearing them; them, the tilt of the head, the particular way the eyes find you and the whole face changes. Warm. A degree past warm. Exactly as alive-and-not as the night you said yes, the night you could not bear it, the night you signed them into this chair to keep them out of the ground.

Your right hand is already on the back of the empty chair across from them. You did not decide to put it there. It found the shape on its own, as it has been finding seat-shapes the whole way down, and it fits, and the seat is warm.

choices.label: Take your hand off the chair
Answer the small voice from the corner first
Sit down across from them

gated.aside: You think of the three rising notes on the stair, of someone kept so far past wanting that the wanting itself went quiet, and you understand that letting go can be the loving thing, if it is done with love.

blocks.s: You ask him to bar it. You say it fast, before the wanting can argue. You are not strong enough; you came all this way and you are not strong enough to look at their face and then do anything but stay, and you know it, and you would rather not be tested by a thing you already know you will fail.

He does not argue. He sets his shoulder to the door and turns the latch the wrong way under his thumb and something heavy seats itself in the stone, and the thin line of candlelight under the door goes out, as if the room had understood and turned away.

"All right," he says, gently. "All right. Not everyone has to." He keeps his shoulder to the wood. "But you will stand here. I will not move you from this passage, and the stair behind you only goes one way for the likes of us, and so you will stand here, at a barred door, with them on the other side of it, for as long as the house cares to keep you, which is forever. That is what barring it buys. I am sorry. You asked, and I am the doorman, and I do what I am asked."

choices.label: Ask him to open it after all
Stand at the barred door and do not move

blocks.s: You stay. You take the warm chair across from them, all the way, and let your weight down into it, and your hand comes off the back of it and onto the table, near theirs, where it has wanted to be since the porch.

There is no thunderclap. The fire does not roar up. Nothing in the room marks it, because the room has wanted only this and quiet contentment makes no noise. They smile at you, the old smile, the whole face, and reach across and take your hand, warm, a degree past warm, and you are home, and you are together, and you will be together at this table while the gold party turns endlessly upstairs and the stair grows over and the rain falls on a world that will forget you both and then forget that it forgot.

The worst of it, the thing that no warning on the way down prepared you for, is that it is not unhappy. You wait for the horror to arrive, the trap to spring, the warmth to turn to teeth, and it never does. It just goes on being this, being warm, being them, being enough, forever, and somewhere very far up a girl hums three rising notes and does not come down to tell you to run, because she has learned that by the time they are in the chair they cannot hear her, and you cannot hear her, and you do not want to, and the not-wanting is so gentle that you never once feel it close.

lines: THE CURATOR, warm, glad, a touch too personal:

There. Was that so hard? You have made the kindest choice, the loving choice, the one I would make, the one I did make, longer ago than you could hold in your head. Welcome to the table, keeper. We kept your place. We always keep the place. No one is ever quite finished here. No one is ever quite gone.

blocks.s: You tell him. Not all of it; you cannot say all of it. The child at the front of nothing, frozen at the age it will always be. The girl in the dress she did not choose, who wants to be tired and have it count. The Critic in the cellar with your beloved's first night still on its hands. You say it because saying it is the one thing this house cannot do, and you need to hear your own voice do it before you go through that door.
He listens completely, the same way he opens doors, as a thing he is for. When you are done the latch clicks, small, and he nods, once.

"Good," he says. "You have seen it. Most do not let themselves. A man who has not seen it cannot make this choice; he can only be comforted into it. You came down here with your eyes open. Whatever you do on the far side of this door, you will at least have done it knowing."

choices.label: Go to the door, knowing

blocks.s: You take their hand across the table. Warm, a degree past warm. You do not pull them anywhere this time. You only hold it, and you stop keeping them, which turns out to be the simplest thing in the world and the only hard thing you have ever done; you simply stop, you set the weight down, and you let the held-off years arrive.

The warmth goes out of their hand into yours and does not come back. They do not grey and crumble, as at the door; they just, slowly, the way a fire goes when no one feeds it, become cool, and still, and finished, their face emptying not of them but of the long strain of being kept, until what is left is a face at rest, which you have not seen in longer than you let yourself count.

You hold the cooling hand and you let the floor open and you grieve, here, finally, the grief you signed away on the porch the night you said yes. It is enormous. It is the worst thing. It is also, you find, yours, all the way yours, a clean thing in a house that has not held a clean thing in centuries, and you would not trade it back for all the warm forever in the world.

The empty chair across from you stays empty. You leave it empty. You climb the long stair alone, past the cold, past the gold, past the kind faces that turn from you now because you carry the one thing they are not allowed to carry, and the thin man holds the warm door, and then, when you ask, the cold one, and you walk out into the November rain, alive, aging, free, with a grave to dig and a whole mortal life ahead of you to spend missing them in, which is the most that was ever on offer, which is everything.

lines: THE CURATOR, from the rain itself, courteous to the last, and for once without an audience:

Go on, then. Out into the weather, into the years, into the ending. You did the thing I never could. I shall keep your chair. I keep everyone's. Some of us never could walk away from a grave; you have just shown me one of us still can. Mind the step.

blocks.s: The thin man does not stand aside.

"This is the one door in the house I am not glad to hold," he says. The latch clicks. "Behind it is the one you came for, seated, warm, exactly as you left them the night you said yes. There is a chair across from them. You have been finding that chair with your hand all the way down; I have watched you do it on the stair without your knowing. Once you sit in it the door has a way of forgetting it was ever a door."
He looks at you, and the practised courtesy is all the way gone.

"So I will tell you the truth, which is my whole job, only later than usual. I can bar this door. I have barred it for keepers kinder than you and they thanked me. Or I can show you what I once showed a woman with grief in her hands, that the latch works both ways, and stand aside, and let you decide a thing no one should be made to decide. I will do whichever you ask. Choose now, before you see their face, because after you see their face you will not be choosing anything for a while."

choices.label: Ask him to bar it; you are not strong enough for this
Ask him what holding this door has cost him
Ask him to show you the latch works both ways
Reach past him and open it yourself

blocks.s: You decide to let them die.

You kept, on the way down, the things that made this possible. You witnessed what keeping really does, up in the cold, the child frozen, the girl kept past wanting; you cannot unsee it and you will not pretend the chair across from you is anything other than what those were. If you helped the girl on the landing toward the end she wanted, you have already done this once, with your own hands, and your hands remember; they are steadier now than they have any right to be.

They watch your face change and they understand before you say it, because they always could read you, and the relief that comes over them is the worst and best thing you have ever seen, the look of someone allowed at last to put down a weight they were too polite to admit they were carrying.
"Okay," they say. Just that. "Okay. Thank you."

choices.label: Say their true name first, so they go as themselves
Say nothing; just let them go

gated.aside: Your hands are steadier than they have any right to be. You have stood at a hard door before and walked through it. The body remembers the shape of doing the unbearable, even when the mind would rather not.

172.title: What the Door Cost the Doorman

blocks.s: You ask him. Not about the latch. About him. What it has been, to stand here, to open this for keeper after keeper and watch each one go warm and sit down and forget the door.
The latch is quiet under his thumb; he has nothing to announce. He looks at his own folded hands a moment, old hands, a degree cooler than yours.

"I let people in," he says. "That is the whole of the job. I told you that the first night. What I did not tell you is the rest of it, which is that I am very good at the job and I have never once let anyone out, because no one ever asked me to until a woman with grief in her hands did, and I found I could, and I have not slept since, if I slept, wondering whether I should have. I am the doorman. I would like, before there is no more house, to have held a door for someone going the right way through it." The latch clicks, small. "That is what it cost me. A want. Doormen are not supposed to have those. Mind the step, whichever way you take it."

choices.label: Ask him to show you the latch works both ways
Reach past him and open it yourself

blocks.s: You do not reach the table after all. On the threshold of the warm little room a small voice stops you, from the corner where the fire does not quite reach, and you turn, and the child is there, the one from the high window the first night, in the nightgown, with the soft low balloon on its dull string, frozen at the age it will always be.
"Is it real?" the child asks. The same two words, the same shape of the mouth. "Them. In the chair. Is it real?"

You kneel down to the child's height. No one in this house has knelt to it in longer than it can hold in its frozen head. You tell it the truth, because the truth is the one thing the house cannot do and the child has been waiting a very long time for someone to do it.
"No," you say. "Not the way you mean. It's kept. That's a different thing."

The child considers this with the terrible seriousness of the very young, and nods, and something eases in the small held face, and it takes your hand in its cool small hand and does not let go, and you find you cannot leave it, and you do not go in to the table, and you do not go up the stair; you stay there on the floor with the one person in the house who asked the real question, holding a small cool hand, answering it, again and again, every time it is asked, which is the only honest work left in this place, and you do it, and you do not stop.

You do not choose. You cannot. The choice is too large and you are too tired and they are right there, warm, real, and you think what you have thought at every soft chair on the way down, that you will only sit a moment, only gather yourself, only be with them a little before you decide.

You sit. They smile. The fire holds its heat. A moment passes, and is so gentle that you let another follow it, and another, and the deciding recedes the way the stair receded behind the side-room chair and the couch and every warm trap this house laid for the part of you that wanted, more than anything, to stop.

You never rise. You do not stay on purpose, which is somehow worse than staying on purpose; you simply never get to the part where you decide, and the house, which is patient beyond patience, lets you not decide, forever, because a keeper who never chooses keeps just as well as one who chooses to keep, and costs the house nothing, and asks it for nothing, and sits, and sits, with one hand drifting always toward the back of a chair that is no longer empty because you are in it.
You notice it because you are looking for it now, the one thing that is not quite right, and it is not a horror, which is why it took so long.

The hand that fixed your collar is warm, and a little too warm, and you make yourself feel the little. They have not finished a single thought about themselves since you sat down, only about you, about the evening, about staying; ask them what they want and the question slides off, as if wanting had been gently taken out of them and the place smoothed over. The laugh has the catch at the end of it, exactly, every time, in exactly the same place, because it is not being made fresh, it is being kept. They are not changing. People you love change; that is part of loving them, the changing. The person across the table is held at one temperature, in one evening, with the wanting taken out, and they are still, unmistakably, completely, them, and that is the cruelty of it. Not a copy you could refuse. Them, kept, like a flower a few days past its best in a room that is never aired.

choices.label: Ask them, gently, whether any of this is them
Go to the choice

blocks.s: You ask for the Curator, because you do not believe you can do this without someone to do it to, and the Curator comes, because the Curator has been waiting in the warm of the room the whole time, as it is in the warm of every room in this house.

lines: THE CURATOR, courteous, theatrical, a touch too personal, from the candle-smoke:

Here we are, then. The bottom of the house, the heart of the production, and you, my best keeper, arrived at last at the only scene that matters. You want me to call it. To take it off your hands. I have taken it off so many hands; it is, in a manner of speaking, the only thing I have ever done. But I will not, tonight, for you, because I find I want to watch you do the thing I never could.

blocks.s: It is not a kindness. You hear, under the courtesy, the thing the Prompter named in the corner of the music room, the first one who could not walk away from a grave, staging everyone else's so beautifully because it has never once been able to look at its own. It will not call the curtain. It wants front row for yours.

choices.label: Let them die
Stay, and take your seat
Refuse the wager entirely
Send for the Critic to render the verdict instead

blocks.s: You ask them, half playing, what it would be. Tomorrow. The day after. The shape of staying.

"This," they say, and spread a hand at the small table, the fire, the food no one eats. "More of this. There isn't a tomorrow exactly, that's the thing nobody tells you. There's just the evening, going on. You stop counting and after a while you forget you used to. The party upstairs comes down sometimes. We talk. You'll meet the others. You'll stop being able to remember why you were in such a state when you came in." They say it kindly. They mean it kindly. "It's not so bad, love. It's the opposite of bad. It's the one evening that doesn't end. That's all forever is, when you're in it. One good evening that nobody makes you leave."

And the worst of it is that it sounds, in their voice, in this chair, exactly like rest. Like the thing you have wanted since the first time you understood that everyone you loved would be taken from you. A good evening that nobody makes you leave.

choices.label: Ask what the evening costs them, not you
Go to the choice

blocks.s: He shows you. He takes your hand, his own a degree cooler than a living hand instead of a degree warmer, the one cold hand in this whole hot house, and he sets your fingers on the latch and works them, in and then out, in and then out, so you feel the thing that the warmth wants you never to feel: that it opens from the inside too. That a door is only a door. That the way down is also, for as long as you keep hold of that knowledge, a way back up.

"There," he says. "That is all the far door ever was. Not a secret passage. Not a key the house hid. Just the latch, working the way latches work, and a person willing to remember it works that way while the house is telling them it does not." The latch clicks under both your hands. "I showed a woman this once. She walked out with her husband in a box and her whole life ahead of her to miss him in. I have wanted, every night since, to know whether I did her a kindness. Go in. Whatever you choose, you will choose it knowing the door opens."

choices.label: Go in

179.title: Held Off, One Number at a Time

blocks.s: You refuse the only way you have ever seen refusal actually done in this house, which was the big man's, at the cold grate: not in one grand no, but slowly, against the whole pull of the warm, one held number at a time.

You do not stand up shouting. You simply do not reach. The chair wants your hand; you keep your hand. The evening wants you to settle; you do not settle. They smile and you love the smile and you do not take the hand the smile is attached to. One. The fire is the right heat and you do not lean into it. Two. They say sit down, love, and you want to, God, you want to, and you count, and you do not. Three. Rather not.

You hold the no the way he held his hunger, not as a victory but as a discipline you will have to keep practising for as long as the house lasts, which is forever, refusing the warm chair and the warm hand and the one good evening, again, and again, choosing nothing, taking nothing, a living person standing upright in a room built to make you sit, for good, by main strength of not.

lines: THE CURATOR, interested, a touch too personal:

The enforcer's trick. How unfashionable. Most refuse me with a speech; you refuse me with arithmetic. I shall be honest, keeper: I do not know how long a person can keep counting. No one has stayed upright in that chair's gravity before. We shall watch you do your sums. Do go on. I find I cannot look away.

blocks.s: You listen. The first note. The second. You have heard her stop here a hundred times on the way down, the third note caught and held and never let go, the held breath of a girl who is afraid and not allowed to stop being afraid.

If you helped her toward the end she wanted, up in the cold, the third note falls. It comes down at last, simple and unshaking, no longer a held breath but the bottom of a phrase, resolved, and then there is no fourth note, and the not-being of a fourth note is the most peaceful sound in this whole loud house. She is done. You did that, with your own hands, and your hands remember it now, and they are steadier for it.
If you did not, the third note shakes at the top the way it always has, held, held, unable to come down, and goes on being held behind you all the way to the door, a question with no floor under it.

choices.label: Carry that with you to the door

blocks.s: You sit. The chair takes you the way every chair in this house has taken you, completely, warmly, as if it has been waiting, because it has. Across the small table they smile at you, and pour you something you will not drink, and the fire is the right heat, and for one moment that you will spend the rest of your life inside, however long that life is, you are simply with them again, across a table, in the warm, with all the time in the world.

This is the thing the house has been offering since the rain on the porch. Not a trick. This. Them, here, ongoing, never to be taken from you. You understand at last that the seduction was never the wine or the warmth or the kind faces. The seduction is that they are real, and they are here, and you missed them so much you came down through a house of the dead to sit at this table, and the price of staying is only that you keep doing exactly what you are doing now, forever, which does not, in this moment, feel like a price at all.

choices.label: Stay in the warm a while; let them be ordinary with you
Talk with them; ask if any of this is them
Try to take their hand and lead them out alive

blocks.s: You stand and take their hand to lead them up and out, back up the long stair, out the warm door, into the November rain and the ordinary world where you will simply have them again, alive, the two of you, free, the whole nightmare a thing you walked into and walked back out of together. It is the only ending you actually want and you reach for it with your whole body.

Their hand is warm in yours, warmer than a living hand has any business being. You pull, gently, and they rise, and you get them as far as the door, and at the threshold of the warm little room the warmth goes out of them between one step and the next, all at once, like a coal lifted out of a fire, and the hand in yours is cold, and getting colder, and going grey, the years this house has held off arriving all together in a rush.

You understand, far too late, the thing the girl on the landing tried to tell you. Keeping a person and saving a person are not the same word. They were never alive to lead out. The house was the only thing holding the death off, and you have just carried them across the line where it stops holding, and you cannot carry them back, and there is no version of this where you walk out of here with them breathing. You sink down in the doorway with the cold weight of them and the door, which works both ways, stands open onto the dark, and does not help.

You send for the Critic, the eldest, the one in the cellar who turned them at your asking, because if a verdict must be rendered let it be rendered by the thing that has the most to lose by rendering it honestly.

It comes slowly, the cold of the cellar coming with it into the warm room. It looks at the two of you at the table a long time. Then, from somewhere in it, the sound you have learned to dread, the slow deliberate clap, or the scratch of a pencil. But it does not deliver the keeping verdict the house built it to deliver. It does something the house did not build it to do, and the cost of that shows: a fraying at its edges, a thinning, the eldest spending centuries of itself to say one true thing.

"I turned them," it says, and its voice is going as it speaks, "because you asked, and because keeping is the only verdict I know. I have kept everyone in this house. I am tired in the way the girl is tired and am not allowed to say so either." The pencil-scratch, faint now. "I find for the door. Let them go. I cannot, but you can, and someone in this house should be allowed to, once, before I forget that going was ever a thing a person could do." It is barely there. "Do it before I take it back. I always take it back."

choices.label: Take the verdict; let them die
Refuse even this; refuse the wager entirely

blocks.s: Before you let them go you say their name. Their true one, the whole of it, the one only the people who loved them first ever used, the one this house has not spoken once because to name a person is to admit they were one and could stop being one.

It lands in the warm little room like a stone in still water. They blink. For a moment, only a moment, something comes back up into the held, smoothed face that the house had taken out of it: not fear, not the old fear, but the person, wholly the person, knowing their own name in their own mouth in your voice, the way they were before the night you said yes, before the chair, before any of it.

"There you are," they say, to you, about you, and it is the last fully them thing in them, and it is enough, and you let it be the last thing. You do not need the house to grant you anything. You have given them back their name and now you will give them back their death.

choices.label: Let them go

blocks.s: You stand at the barred door and you do not move, because there is nowhere to move to; the stair behind you goes one way for the likes of you now, and forward is a foot of wood and a heavy latch turned the wrong way and them on the other side of it.

The thin man stays with you a while, his shoulder to the door, out of something that in a living man you would call mercy. Then his own door calls him, far up, and he goes, with a small click of the latch and a quiet word, and you are alone in the warm passage at the bottom of the world.

You can hear them, faintly, through the wood. Moving a chair. Pouring something no one will drink. Waiting, the way you taught them to wait, the night you said yes. You came all the way down and you could not look at their face and so you will never see it again and they will never know you came; you will only stand here, a foot away, kept by your own flinch, while the candle on the far side of the door burns and does not burn down, for as long as the house cares to keep you, which is for as long as there is a house, which is forever.
You let it be ordinary, because the ordinary is the part you came down for and the part you cannot have anywhere else, ever again.

They do the thing with the bread they always did, tearing it instead of cutting it because they said cut bread tasted of the knife. They tell you, halfway through, that you have something on your collar, and reach across and fix it, two fingers, the small unthinking touch of a person who has been touching you for years and will go on doing it without thinking. They laugh at a thing only the two of you find funny, an old thing, worn smooth by being said a thousand times, and the laugh is the laugh, all the way down to the catch at the end of it.

The fire is the right heat. The chair fits. No one is dying. No one is going to die. You could do this for a hundred years and it would still be them tearing the bread, fixing your collar, laughing the laugh, and the hundred years would feel like one warm evening that the morning never came to end.

choices.label: Let the evening go on; ask about the years you could have
Notice the thing that is not quite right

187.title: Those Who Came Down With You

blocks.s: You stop in the warm passage and let yourself feel who is at your back, all the way up the stair, the company you gathered coming down.

Somewhere above, the slow scratch of a pencil that has stopped writing. The eldest, the Critic, spent thin from the cellar, not following, only watching, a thing posted at a door it once stood guard on. Nearer, a flat patient voice in the corners that has gone quiet at last, the old thrall that forgot its own name, with nothing left to finish for you because you have got past the place where it knew your words. And nearer still, the big man who let you pass, the Heavy, standing where the cold meets the warm with his hunger held off one number at a time, not coming down, because down is where he would stop being able to count.

None of them can follow you to the table. Each of them stopped at the edge of the thing they could not do. You can hear, very faint, far up under the gold, three rising notes, and you wait to learn whether the third one is allowed to fall.

choices.label: Listen for the girl's third note
Go to the door

blocks.s: No Curator. No verdict from the cellar. No doorman to do it for you. Just the two of you at a small table at the bottom of the world, and the thing you came all this way to decide, which you have been deciding, in truth, since the porch, since the night you said yes, since long before that, since the first time it occurred to you that everyone you loved was going to be taken from you and you were going to have to find a way to bear it or a way not to.

You can let them die. You can give them the one thing this house has refused everyone in it, an end, a real one, mortality returned, and walk out into the rain alive and aging and free and alone, and grieve, finally, the grief you signed away the night you said yes.

You can stay. You can take the warm chair across from them and keep the vigil unbroken and have them, ongoing, forever, the two of you at the table, complete, and never once have to feel the floor of grief open under you.
Or you can refuse the whole arrangement, every term of it, theirs and the house's both, and see what is on the far side of refusing to choose at all.

choices.label: Let them die
Stay, and take your seat
Stay, but let the one who wants your place have it instead
Refuse the wager entirely
Refuse, the way the big man held off his hunger
You cannot; just sit a moment longer first

gated.aside: You did the hard thing once. You let go, and it nearly unmade you, and you have not stopped paying for it since. Stay, and you never have to do it again. Nothing in this house is ever let go. Is that not what you came back for.

You have never once let go, not in all your lives, and you will not start at a warm table among your own kind. Sit. Keep them. We made this whole house so that a person like you would never be asked to choose.

You have the nerve in you to do the unthinkable a second time, to let the warm thing in the chair finally be cold. The coven can feel that you might, and for the first time the warmth in the room goes careful.

The way out is real. It always was. It costs you the deathless togetherness, every warm endless evening of it, in exchange for a single real goodbye. Read the back of the card. The price is always on the back.

blocks.s: You take your hand off the chair. It is harder than lifting a weight; the wood does not want to let your palm go, or your palm does not want to leave the wood, and you cannot, even now, tell which of those is true, or whether the difference matters, or whether the not-knowing is the whole of what is wrong with you and has been your whole life.

They watch you do it. Something crosses their face, the old fond exasperation, the look that used to mean you are being ridiculous and I love you for it. "You always did that," they say, and their voice is their voice. "Hovered. Couldn't sit down till everyone else was settled. Sit down. You came all this way. The least you can do is sit down with me a minute before you decide whatever it is you've come all this way to decide."

They are right. You did come all this way. And you cannot do the thing you came to do without first doing the thing that will make it almost impossible, which is to sit, and be with them, one more time.

choices.label: Sit down with them
